fact: The kanji 愛 is one of the most famous Japanese characters worldwide. Unlike the casual 'love' often used in English, 愛 usually represents deep affection, devotion, and lasting emotional bonds.
memory: Imagine a heart hidden inside a person who carefully protects it because it contains their deepest love. The complex shape of 愛 reminds us that true love is powerful and layered.

sentence.japanese: 家族への 愛 は とても 大切です。
sentence.reading: かぞくへの あい は とても たいせつです。
sentence.romaji: kazoku e no ai wa totemo taisetsu desu
sentence.english: Love for family is very important.

fact: 位 is used everywhere in Japan, from school rankings to company job titles and even the Emperor's throne position.
memory: A person (亻) standing in one place (立) has their own position or rank. Think of someone proudly standing on a podium.

sentence.japanese: 彼は一位になりました。
sentence.reading: かれは いちい に なりました。

fact: Teachers often praise children by saying 『えらい！』 meaning 'Well done!' or 'Good job!'.
memory: A person (亻) who rises above others becomes great and admirable.

sentence.japanese: 彼は偉い先生です。
sentence.reading: かれは えらい せんせいです。

fact: The common word 易しい means 'easy', while 優しい (same pronunciation) means 'kind'. They sound identical but use different kanji.
memory: Imagine the difficult parts melting away until everything becomes easy.

sentence.japanese: この問題は易しいです。
sentence.reading: この もんだい は やさしいです。

fact: 違 is one of the most common kanji in everyday conversations because Japanese people often say 『違います』 ('That's different' or 'That's not correct').
memory: Walking (辶) down the wrong path leads somewhere different.

sentence.japanese: 答えが違います。
sentence.reading: こたえ が ちがいます。

fact: The kanji 育 appears in many everyday words related to education, childcare, and personal growth. It combines the ideas of raising and developing.
memory: Imagine carefully raising a child until they grow into a healthy adult. 育 reminds us that growth takes time and care.

sentence.japanese: 子どもは元気に育っています。
sentence.reading: こども は げんき に そだっています。
sentence.english: The child is growing up healthy.

fact: 因 is commonly used in formal Japanese to explain causes and reasons, especially in newspapers and official documents.
memory: Something trapped inside a box becomes the cause of everything that happens afterward.

sentence.japanese: 事故の原因を調べています。
sentence.reading: じこの げんいん を しらべています。
sentence.english: They are investigating the cause of the accident.

fact: Swimming is a compulsory activity in many Japanese elementary schools, so 泳 is one of the first sports kanji children learn.
memory: Water (氵) plus movement makes someone swim across the river.

sentence.japanese: 私は毎週プールで泳ぎます。
sentence.reading: わたし は まいしゅう プール で およぎます。
sentence.romaji: watashi wa maishuu puuru de oyogimasu
sentence.english: I swim at the pool every week.

fact: 越 appears in common expressions like 引っ越し (moving house), one of the most useful everyday N3 vocabulary words.
memory: Imagine running over a mountain to cross to the other side.

sentence.japanese: 来月、新しい家へ引っ越します。
sentence.reading: らいげつ、あたらしい いえ へ ひっこします。
sentence.english: Next month, I'll move to a new house.

fact: 園 appears in many familiar place names such as 公園 (park), 幼稚園 (kindergarten), and 動物園 (zoo).
memory: Imagine a large fence surrounding a beautiful garden full of trees and flowers.

sentence.japanese: 子どもたちは公園で遊んでいます。
sentence.reading: こどもたち は こうえん で あそんでいます。
sentence.english: The children are playing in the park.

fact: The kanji 演 is commonly seen in words related to performances, speeches, and acting. It's especially familiar in 演劇 (theater) and 演奏 (musical performance).
memory: Imagine an actor performing on a stage surrounded by flowing water effects. The water radical (氵) reminds you of a performance that flows smoothly.

sentence.japanese: 彼は舞台で演じました。
sentence.reading: かれ は ぶたい で えんじました。

fact: 煙 is used in many everyday words such as 禁煙 (no smoking) and 喫煙 (smoking), which you'll often see on signs across Japan.
memory: Imagine smoke rising from a fire and filling the air. The fire radical (火) helps you remember smoke.

sentence.japanese: 煙が空へ上がっています。
sentence.reading: けむり が そら へ あがっています。
sentence.english: Smoke is rising into the sky.

fact: 押 is one of the most practical kanji in Japan because elevator buttons and automatic doors often display 押 ('Push').
memory: Imagine using your hand (扌) to firmly push a heavy door open.

sentence.japanese: このボタンを押してください。
sentence.reading: この ボタン を おしてください。

fact: The city of Yokohama (横浜) begins with this kanji. It also appears in everyday words like 横断歩道 (pedestrian crossing).
memory: Picture a long tree lying sideways across the road. It reminds you of something horizontal.

sentence.japanese: 道を横に渡ります。
sentence.reading: みち を よこ に わたります。

fact: 王 appears in many famous words such as 王様 (king), 王子 (prince), and even the title of the popular manga 『ONE PIECE』 as 海賊王 ('Pirate King').
memory: The three horizontal lines represent heaven, earth, and people, while the vertical line connects them all. The one who unites everything is the king.

sentence.japanese: 王様は国を治めました。
sentence.reading: おうさま は くに を おさめました。

fact: 化 appears in many everyday words such as 文化 (culture), 変化 (change), and 化学 (chemistry), making it one of the most useful kanji for understanding abstract concepts.
memory: Imagine a person (亻) magically changing into something else. 化 literally represents transformation.

sentence.japanese: 町は大きく変化しました。
sentence.reading: まち は おおきく へんか しました。

kunyomi: くわえる (kuwaeru), くわわる (kuwawaru)
fact: 加 is frequently used in everyday words like 参加 (participation) and 増加 (increase), making it a practical kanji for business and daily conversations.
memory: Imagine using your strength (力) to add another item onto a pile.

sentence.japanese: 新しいメンバーが参加しました。
sentence.reading: あたらしい メンバー が さんか しました。

fact: Japanese school subjects almost always end with 科, such as 理科 (science) and 外科 (surgery).
memory: Imagine dividing knowledge into different branches or subjects. Each branch becomes its own 科.

sentence.japanese: 私は理科が好きです。
sentence.reading: わたし は りか が すきです。
sentence.romaji: watashi wa rika ga suki desu

kunyomi: はたす (hatasu), はてる (hateru), はて (hate)
fact: 果 originally meant 'fruit' but later came to represent the 'result' or 'outcome' of an action, just as fruit is the result of a tree's growth.
memory: Imagine a tree finally producing fruit after months of care. Fruit is the result of hard work.

sentence.japanese: 試験の結果が出ました。
sentence.reading: しけん の けっか が でました。
sentence.english: The exam results have been announced.

kunyomi: すぎる (sugiru), すごす (sugosu), あやまち (ayamachi)
fact: 過 is everywhere in daily Japanese, appearing in words like 過去 (past), 過ぎる (to exceed), and 通過 (to pass through).
memory: Imagine walking (辶) past the finish line. You've gone beyond or exceeded the limit.

sentence.japanese: 時間が早く過ぎました。
sentence.reading: じかん が はやく すぎました。

kunyomi: とく (toku), とける (tokeru), とかす (tokasu), わかる (wakaru)
fact: 解 appears in many everyday words such as 理解 (understanding), 解決 (solution), and 正解 (correct answer). It's one of the most useful kanji for students.
memory: Imagine carefully untangling a complicated knot. Once it's untied, you finally understand the solution.

sentence.japanese: 先生の説明がよく理解できました。
sentence.reading: せんせい の せつめい が よく りかい できました。
sentence.romaji: sensei no setsumei ga yoku rikai dekimashita
sentence.english: I understood the teacher's explanation well.

fact: 回 is one of the most frequently used kanji in Japanese. It's used to count occurrences (一回, 二回), describe rotation, and indicate going around.
memory: Imagine walking around a square over and over again. Every complete circle is one 回.

sentence.japanese: この映画は三回見ました。
sentence.reading: この えいが は さんかい みました。
sentence.english: I watched this movie three times.

fact: Although everyone says みんな every day, many learners don't realize it's written with the kanji 皆.
memory: Imagine everyone standing together in one big group. That's 皆.

sentence.japanese: 皆さん、おはようございます。
sentence.reading: みなさん、おはようございます。

fact: Japan has a long tradition of 絵巻 (picture scrolls) and 浮世絵 (ukiyo-e), famous woodblock prints that inspired artists like Van Gogh.
memory: Imagine colorful threads (糸) woven together to create a beautiful picture.

sentence.japanese: 妹は絵を描くのが好きです。
sentence.reading: いもうと は え を かく の が すきです。
sentence.romaji: imouto wa e o kaku no ga suki desu
sentence.english: My younger sister likes drawing pictures.

fact: 害 is commonly seen in news reports about natural disasters and pollution, such as 災害 (disaster) and 公害 (pollution).
memory: Imagine a roof protecting a house, but something harmful breaks through and causes damage.

sentence.japanese: 台風で大きな被害が出ました。
sentence.reading: たいふう で おおきな ひがい が でました。
sentence.romaji: taifuu de ookina higai ga demashita

fact: 格 appears in many common words such as 性格 (personality), 合格 (passing an exam), and 価格 (price), making it one of the most useful N3 kanji.
memory: Imagine a tree (木) being measured against a standard. If it meets the required standard, it has the right 格.

sentence.japanese: 試験に合格しました。
sentence.reading: しけん に ごうかく しました。

kunyomi: たしか (tashika), たしかめる (tashikameru)
fact: You'll constantly hear 確認してください ('Please confirm') in Japan, whether using ATMs, online forms, or train ticket machines.
memory: Imagine checking a solid stone carefully until you're certain it's real. That's 確.

sentence.japanese: 予約を確認してください。
sentence.reading: よやく を かくにん してください。

kunyomi: おぼえる (oboeru), さめる (sameru), さます (samasu)
fact: 覚える is one of the first verbs Japanese learners master because it means 'to memorize'—something every student does daily.
memory: Imagine opening your eyes and suddenly remembering everything you studied.

sentence.japanese: 新しい漢字を覚えました。
sentence.reading: あたらしい かんじ を おぼえました。

fact: 掛 is used in many everyday expressions, such as 電話を掛ける (to make a phone call) and 壁に掛ける (to hang on a wall).
memory: Imagine using your hand (扌) to hang a picture carefully on the wall.

words.reading: でんわ を かける
こえ を かける

sentence.japanese: 友達に電話を掛けました。
sentence.reading: ともだち に でんわ を かけました。

kunyomi: わる (waru), われる (wareru), わり (wari)
fact: Japan has a famous custom called 割り勘 (warikan), where everyone splits the bill equally after eating together.
memory: Imagine cutting a cake into equal pieces so everyone gets their share. That's 割.

sentence.japanese: 今日は割り勘にしましょう。
sentence.reading: きょう は わりかん に しましょう。

fact: 活 appears in many everyday words such as 生活 (daily life), 活動 (activity), and 活気 (liveliness). It's one of the most practical N3 kanji.
memory: Water (氵) gives life to everything. Wherever there is water, things become active and alive.

sentence.japanese: 日本での生活は楽しいです。
sentence.reading: にほん で の せいかつ は たのしいです。
sentence.romaji: nihon de no seikatsu wa tanoshii desu

fact: 完 is frequently seen in books, manga, and TV series. The character 完 at the end simply means 'The End' or 'Completed'.
memory: Imagine putting the final roof on a house. Once the roof is finished, the entire building is complete.

sentence.japanese: 宿題はもう完成しました。
sentence.reading: しゅくだい は もう かんせい しました。

fact: 官 is used in words related to government, such as 大使館 (embassy), 警官 (police officer), and 官僚 (bureaucrat).
memory: Imagine a government official working inside an official building under a large roof.

sentence.japanese: 警官に道を聞きました。
sentence.reading: けいかん に みち を ききました。
sentence.english: I asked a police officer for directions.

fact: 感 appears in many emotional words such as 感謝 (gratitude), 感情 (emotion), and 感動 (being deeply moved).
memory: Imagine your heart reacting strongly to everything around you. That's what 感 represents—a feeling deep inside.

sentence.japanese: 先生に感謝しています。
sentence.reading: せんせい に かんしゃ しています。
sentence.english: I am grateful to my teacher.

fact: One of the most useful expressions for foreigners in Japan is 『日本の生活に慣れました』 ('I've gotten used to life in Japan').
memory: Your heart (忄) becomes comfortable after doing something many times. Eventually, it becomes a habit.

sentence.japanese: 日本での生活に慣れました。
sentence.reading: にほん で の せいかつ に なれました。
sentence.romaji: nihon de no seikatsu ni naremashita
sentence.english: I've gotten used to living in Japan.

fact: 観 appears in many travel-related words such as 観光 (sightseeing) and 観客 (audience). If you visit Japan, you'll probably hear 観光 every day.
memory: Imagine standing at a high viewpoint carefully observing everything around you. That's exactly what 観 means.

sentence.japanese: 京都へ観光に行きました。
sentence.reading: きょうと へ かんこう に いきました。

kunyomi: せき (seki), かかわる (kakawaru), かんする (kansuru)
fact: 関 is extremely common in everyday Japanese. It appears in 関係 (relationship), 関心 (interest), and 関西 (Kansai region).
memory: Imagine a large gate controlling who can enter. Whoever passes through becomes connected to what's inside.

sentence.japanese: 日本文化に関心があります。
sentence.reading: にほんぶんか に かんしん が あります。
sentence.romaji: nihon bunka ni kanshin ga arimasu
sentence.english: I am interested in Japanese culture.

fact: Japanese people write their wishes on wooden plaques called 絵馬 (ema) at shrines. These wishes are expressed using the kanji 願.
memory: Imagine holding your hands together and making a heartfelt wish. That's the feeling behind 願.

sentence.japanese: 家族の幸せを願っています。
sentence.reading: かぞく の しあわせ を ねがっています。
sentence.english: I wish for my family's happiness.

kunyomi: あぶない (abunai), あやうい (ayaui)
fact: The warning sign 危険 ('Danger') is found everywhere in Japan—from train stations to construction sites and hiking trails.
memory: Imagine someone standing on the edge of a steep cliff. One more step could be dangerous.

sentence.japanese: その場所は危険です。
sentence.reading: その ばしょ は きけん です。

fact: In Japan, the character 喜 is often used on wedding decorations because it symbolizes happiness and celebration.
memory: Imagine someone smiling so widely they can't stop celebrating. That's the feeling of 喜.

sentence.japanese: 家族はとても喜びました。
sentence.reading: かぞく は とても よろこびました。

fact: 寄 is commonly used in everyday Japanese when talking about stopping somewhere. You'll often hear 『帰りにコンビニへ寄ります』 ('I'll stop by the convenience store on my way home').
memory: Imagine walking toward a house to stop by for a quick visit. You are getting closer or approaching it.

sentence.japanese: 仕事の帰りにスーパーへ寄ります。
sentence.reading: しごと の かえり に スーパー へ よります。
sentence.romaji: shigoto no kaeri ni suupaa e yorimasu
sentence.english: I'll stop by the supermarket after work.

fact: Although people usually write いくつ in hiragana, its original kanji is 幾. You'll still see it in formal writing and words like 幾何学 (geometry).
memory: Imagine counting many small objects until you lose track. 'How many are there?' That's 幾.

sentence.japanese: 今日は幾つ漢字を勉強しましたか。
sentence.reading: きょう は いくつ かんじ を べんきょう しましたか。
sentence.romaji: kyou wa ikutsu kanji o benkyou shimashita ka
sentence.english: How many kanji did you study today?

fact: 期 appears in many useful words such as 期間 (period), 学期 (school term), and 期待 (expectation).
memory: Imagine marking important dates on a calendar. Each marked section is a period of time.

sentence.japanese: 夏休みの期間は一か月です。
sentence.reading: なつやすみ の きかん は いっかげつ です。
sentence.romaji: natsuyasumi no kikan wa ikkagetsu desu
sentence.english: The summer vacation period is one month.

fact: 機 is everywhere in modern Japanese. It appears in 飛行機 (airplane), 自動販売機 (vending machine), and 機会 (opportunity).
memory: Imagine a complex wooden machine full of moving gears working perfectly together.

sentence.japanese: 飛行機で大阪へ行きます。
sentence.reading: ひこうき で おおさか へ いきます。
sentence.english: I'll go to Osaka by airplane.

fact: 規 appears in words like 規則 (rules), 規定 (regulations), and 正規 (official). Japanese schools and companies frequently use this kanji.
memory: Imagine using a ruler to draw perfect straight lines. Rules and standards keep everything organized.

sentence.japanese: 学校の規則を守ってください。
sentence.reading: がっこう の きそく を まもって ください。
sentence.romaji: gakkou no kisoku o mamotte kudasai

fact: The kanji 記 appears in many everyday words such as 日記 (diary), 記事 (article), and 記録 (record). Japanese students commonly keep a 日記 for writing practice.
memory: Imagine using words (言) to write something down so you'll never forget it. That's 記.

sentence.japanese: 毎日日記を書いています。
sentence.reading: まいにち にっき を かいています。
sentence.english: I write a diary every day.

fact: 疑 is commonly used in news and detective stories. Words like 疑問 (question), 容疑者 (suspect), and 疑う (to doubt) are very common.
memory: Imagine someone scratching their head because they're not sure whether something is true. That's 疑.

sentence.japanese: 彼の話を少し疑っています。
sentence.reading: かれ の は を すこし うたがっています。
sentence.romaji: kare no hanashi o sukoshi utagatteimasu
sentence.english: I doubt his story a little.

fact: 議 appears in many formal words such as 会議 (meeting), 議会 (assembly), and 議論 (discussion). It's frequently seen in business Japanese.
memory: Imagine many people using words (言) to discuss important issues around a conference table.

sentence.japanese: 午後に会議があります。
sentence.reading: ごご に かいぎ が あります。
sentence.english: There is a meeting this afternoon.

fact: Japan is famous for its customer service, known as おもて. The kanji 客 appears in words like お客様 (customer), 乗客 (passenger), and 観客 (audience).
memory: Imagine a guest entering a house with a roof over their head. Guests are always welcomed inside.

sentence.japanese: 多くのお客様が来ました。
sentence.reading: おおく の おきゃくさま が きました。

fact: 吸 is commonly used in words related to breathing and smoking, such as 呼吸 (breathing) and 喫煙 (smoking).
memory: Imagine taking a deep breath and pulling fresh air into your lungs. You're inhaling, or 吸う.

sentence.japanese: 深く息を吸ってください。
sentence.reading: ふかく いき を すって ください。

fact: 求 appears in many everyday words related to asking, searching, or demanding, such as 要求 (request), 求人 (job recruitment), and 求婚 (marriage proposal).
memory: Imagine someone stretching both hands forward, desperately seeking or requesting something. Those reaching hands remind you of 求 — to seek or request.

sentence.japanese: 彼は新しい仕事を求めています。
sentence.reading: かれ は あたらしい しごと を もとめて います。
sentence.romaji: kare wa atarashii shigoto o motomete imasu
sentence.english: He is looking for a new job.

fact: 球 is used for almost every ball sport in Japanese, including 野球 (baseball), 地球 (Earth), and 球場 (baseball stadium).
memory: The king (王) proudly holds a perfectly round jewel that shines like a ball. That sparkling sphere reminds you of 球 — a ball or sphere.

sentence.japanese: 弟は野球が大好きです。
sentence.reading: おとうと は やきゅう が だいすき です。
sentence.romaji: otouto wa yakyuu ga daisuki desu

fact: 給 appears in many work-related words such as 給料 (salary), 支給 (payment), and 配給 (distribution).
memory: Imagine someone handing supplies to everyone in a long line. They keep giving and supplying things without stopping. That's 給.

sentence.japanese: 今日は給料日です。
sentence.reading: きょう は きゅうりょうび です。

fact: 居 is found in many common words such as 居酒屋 (Japanese pub), 住居 (residence), and 居間 (living room).
memory: Picture yourself comfortably sitting inside your home, relaxing without moving. When you stay or reside somewhere, you're 居る.

sentence.japanese: 猫は庭にいます。
sentence.reading: ねこ は にわ に います。
sentence.english: The cat is in the garden.

fact: 許 is commonly seen in words like 許可 (permission), 特許 (patent), and 許す (to forgive), all connected to allowing or approving something.
memory: Imagine a strict teacher finally saying, 'Okay, you're allowed.' That moment of permission helps you remember 許 — to permit or allow.

sentence.japanese: 先生は外出を許しました。
sentence.reading: せんせい は がいしゅつ を ゆるしました。
sentence.english: The teacher gave permission to go out.

fact: 供 appears in many common words such as 子供 (child), 提供 (provide), and 供養 (memorial offering). Although 子供 is extremely common, the original meaning of 供 is 'to serve' or 'to offer.'
memory: Imagine a servant respectfully carrying gifts with both hands to offer them to a king. That act of serving and supplying helps you remember 供.

sentence.japanese: この店は無料で水を提供します。
sentence.reading: この みせ は むりょう で みず を ていきょう します。
sentence.romaji: kono mise wa muryou de mizu o teikyou shimasu
sentence.english: This restaurant provides water for free.

fact: 共 is used in words expressing sharing or togetherness, such as 共同 (cooperation), 共通 (common), and 公共 (public).
memory: Picture two friends walking side by side, sharing everything together. Whenever people act together, think of 共.

sentence.japanese: 私たちは共に働いています。
sentence.reading: わたしたち は ともに はたらいて います。

kunyomi: おそれる (osoreru), おそろしい (osoroshii)
fact: 恐 appears in both everyday and formal Japanese. 恐れる means 'to fear,' while the polite phrase 恐れ入ります literally means 'I am overwhelmed' and is often used to express gratitude or apology.
memory: Your heart (心) starts pounding because you're terrified. A fearful heart is the key to remembering 恐.

sentence.japanese: 彼は失敗を恐れません。
sentence.reading: かれ は しっぱい を おそれません。

fact: 局 appears in many government and business organizations, such as 郵便局 (post office), 放送局 (broadcasting station), and 税務局 (tax bureau).
memory: Imagine a small government office where every official works inside one building. That office is a 局.

sentence.japanese: 郵便局は駅の近くにあります。
sentence.reading: ゆうびんきょく は えき の ちかく に あります。
sentence.romaji: yuubinkyoku wa eki no chikaku ni arimasu
sentence.english: The post office is near the station.

fact: 曲 has two major meanings in Japanese. It can mean 'to bend' or 'curve,' and it also means 'a musical piece,' as in 名曲 (masterpiece) or 新曲 (new song).
memory: Imagine a road that suddenly bends into a curve, while someone nearby plays a catchy tune. Both the curved road and the song remind you of 曲.

sentence.japanese: 次の角を右に曲がってください。
sentence.reading: つぎ の かど を みぎ に まがって ください。
sentence.romaji: tsugi no kado o migi ni magatte kudasai
sentence.english: Please turn right at the next corner.

kunyomi: つとめる (tsutomeru), つとまる (tsutomaru)
fact: 勤 is commonly seen in workplace vocabulary such as 勤務 (work duty), 通勤 (commuting), and 勤勉 (diligence). It's one of the most useful kanji for working adults in Japan.
memory: Imagine someone showing up to work every single day without missing a shift. Their dedication and hard work represent 勤.

sentence.japanese: 私は東京で勤務しています。
sentence.reading: わたし は とうきょう で きんむ して います。
sentence.romaji: watashi wa Toukyou de kinmu shite imasu

kunyomi: くるしい (kurushii), くるしむ (kurushimu), にがい (nigai), にがる (nigaru)
fact: Besides meaning suffering, 苦 also means 'bitter.' That's why 苦い is used to describe bitter coffee, dark chocolate, and certain medicines.
memory: Imagine tasting an extremely bitter medicine while struggling through a difficult day. Both the bitter taste and suffering remind you of 苦.

sentence.japanese: 私は数学が苦手です。
sentence.reading: わたし は すうがく が にがて です。
sentence.romaji: watashi wa suugaku ga nigate desu

fact: 具 is widely used for tools and equipment, such as 道具 (tool), 家具 (furniture), and 文具 (stationery).
memory: Imagine opening a toolbox filled with every tool you could ever need. That complete set of equipment reminds you of 具.

sentence.japanese: 新しい家具を買いました。
sentence.reading: あたらしい かぐ を かいました。

fact: 偶 appears in words like 偶然 (by chance), 偶数 (even number), and 配偶者 (spouse). The core idea is something occurring as a pair or by coincidence.
memory: Imagine unexpectedly meeting your best friend while traveling overseas. That lucky coincidence is 偶然, built from 偶.

sentence.japanese: 駅で友達に偶然会いました。
sentence.reading: えき で ともだち に ぐうぜん あいました。
sentence.romaji: eki de tomodachi ni guuzen aimashita
sentence.english: I met my friend at the station by chance.

fact: In Japan, people remove their 靴 before entering homes, temples, traditional inns, many schools, and some restaurants. Wearing outdoor shoes indoors is considered bad manners.
memory: Imagine neatly placing your shoes at the entrance before stepping onto a clean tatami floor. Those shoes remind you of 靴.

sentence.japanese: 玄関で靴を脱いでください。
sentence.reading: げんかん で くつ を ぬいで ください。
sentence.romaji: genkan de kutsu o nuide kudasai
sentence.english: Please take off your shoes at the entrance.

fact: 君 is commonly used as the honorific suffix ～君 (-kun), often used for boys, younger colleagues, or close friends. Historically, it also meant 'lord' or 'ruler.'
memory: Imagine a young prince everyone respectfully calls 'Kimi-sama.' A prince or someone you're addressing as 'you' helps you remember 君.

sentence.japanese: 君は日本語が上手ですね。
sentence.reading: きみ は にほんご が じょうず です ね。
sentence.romaji: kimi wa nihongo ga jouzu desu ne

fact: 係 is frequently seen on signs in Japan, such as 案内係 (information desk), 受付係 (reception staff), and 会計係 (cashier).
memory: Imagine someone wearing a badge that says 'Staff.' They're responsible for helping everyone. That responsible person is a 係.

sentence.japanese: 係の人に聞いてください。
sentence.reading: かかり の ひと に きいて ください。
sentence.romaji: kakari no hito ni kiite kudasai
sentence.english: Please ask the person in charge.

fact: 形 appears in many common words such as 人形 (doll), 図形 (geometric figure), and 形容詞 (adjective, literally 'shape-describing word').
memory: Imagine molding clay into different shapes. Every time the clay changes form, think of 形.

sentence.japanese: 雲の形がおもしろいです。
sentence.reading: くも の かたち が おもしろい です。
sentence.romaji: kumo no katachi ga omoshiroi desu
sentence.english: The shape of the clouds is interesting.

fact: 景 is found in words describing scenery and landscapes, such as 景色 (scenery), 夜景 (night view), and 景気 (economic conditions, originally referring to the 'state of things').
memory: Imagine standing on top of a mountain enjoying a breathtaking view stretching to the horizon. That beautiful scenery is 景.

sentence.japanese: 山からの景色はとてもきれいです。
sentence.reading: やま から の けしき は とても きれい です。
sentence.romaji: yama kara no keshiki wa totemo kirei desu
sentence.english: The view from the mountain is very beautiful.

kunyomi: へる (heru), たつ (tatsu), たてる (tateru)
fact: 経 is one of the most useful business kanji in Japanese. It appears in words like 経済 (economy), 経験 (experience), 経営 (management), and 経由 (via).
memory: Imagine years passing as someone gains experience and eventually manages a company. Time passing and managing both point to 経.

sentence.japanese: 日本で働いた経験があります。
sentence.reading: にほん で はたらいた けいけん が あります。
sentence.romaji: nihon de hataraita keiken ga arimasu
sentence.english: I have experience working in Japan.

fact: 警 is most commonly seen in 警察 (police), 警告 (warning), and 警報 (alarm). Japan's emergency alerts for earthquakes and typhoons often include the word 警報.
memory: Imagine a police officer loudly warning everyone through a megaphone before danger arrives. That warning reminds you of 警.

sentence.japanese: 警察に電話してください。
sentence.reading: けいさつ に でんわ して ください。

fact: 迎 appears in common expressions such as 出迎え (meeting someone on arrival), 歓迎 (welcome), and 迎える (to welcome or greet).
memory: Imagine running to the airport entrance to happily welcome your best friend. Greeting someone with open arms is 迎える.

sentence.japanese: 駅で友達を迎えます。
sentence.reading: えき で ともだち を むかえます。
sentence.english: I will meet my friend at the station.

fact: 欠 is used in many everyday words such as 欠席 (absence), 欠点 (weak point), and 不可欠 (indispensable).
memory: Imagine a puzzle with one piece missing. That empty gap reminds you of 欠 — something lacking or absent.

sentence.japanese: 今日は会議を欠席します。
sentence.reading: きょう は かいぎ を けっせき します。
sentence.romaji: kyou wa kaigi o kesseki shimasu
sentence.english: I will be absent from today's meeting.

fact: 決 is one of the most frequently used N3 kanji, appearing in words like 決定 (decision), 解決 (solution), and 決勝 (final match).
memory: Imagine standing at a crossroads and finally making up your mind. The moment you choose a direction is 決める.

sentence.japanese: 旅行の日程を決めました。
sentence.reading: りょこう の にってい を きめました。

fact: 件 is widely used as a counter for incidents and matters. You'll often see it in words like 事件 (incident), 条件 (condition), and 用件 (business or errand).
memory: Imagine a detective organizing many case files on a desk. Every file is one 'case' or 'matter'—that's 件.

sentence.japanese: 今日は大事な用件があります。
sentence.reading: きょう は だいじな ようけん が あります。
sentence.romaji: kyou wa daijina youken ga arimasu

fact: 権 appears in many important legal and political words such as 人権 (human rights), 権利 (rights), and 政権 (political administration). It represents power, authority, or legal rights.
memory: Imagine a king sitting on a giant wooden throne, holding the power to make decisions. That authority reminds you of 権.

sentence.japanese: 誰にも自由に話す権利があります。
sentence.reading: だれ にも じゆう に はなす けんり が あります。
sentence.romaji: dare ni mo jiyuu ni hanasu kenri ga arimasu
sentence.english: Everyone has the right to speak freely.

fact: 険 is commonly used in words like 危険 (danger), 保険 (insurance), and 険しい (steep or rugged). Although 保険 means 'insurance,' the original idea is protection from danger.
memory: Imagine climbing a dangerously steep mountain where one wrong step could send you falling. That dangerous cliff reminds you of 険.

sentence.japanese: この道は危険です。
sentence.reading: この みち は きけん です。

fact: 原 is used in many important words such as 原因 (cause), 原料 (raw material), and 草原 (grassland). Its original meaning refers to an open plain or something at its source.
memory: Imagine standing in a vast open field where a river begins to flow. That original source reminds you of 原.

sentence.japanese: 事故の原因を調べています。
sentence.reading: じこ の げんいん を しらべて います。
sentence.romaji: jiko no genin o shirabete imasu
sentence.english: They are investigating the cause of the accident.

kunyomi: あらわれる (arawareru), あらわす (arawasu)
fact: 現 appears in everyday words like 現在 (present), 現金 (cash), and 現実 (reality). The common idea is something that exists or appears in front of you now.
memory: Imagine a magician making something suddenly appear right before your eyes. Whatever appears in the present is 現.

sentence.japanese: 現在東京に住んでいます。
sentence.reading: げんざい とうきょう に すんで います。

fact: 限 is frequently used in words like 制限 (restriction), 期限 (deadline), and 無限 (infinity). The kanji always relates to setting or removing limits.
memory: Imagine reaching a tall fence with a sign that says 'Do Not Go Beyond This Point.' That boundary reminds you of 限.

sentence.japanese: 提出期限は明日です。
sentence.reading: ていしゅつ きげん は あした です。

fact: 呼 appears in many everyday words such as 呼ぶ (to call), 呼吸 (breathing), and 呼び出す (to call someone out). The original idea is using your mouth to call or breathe.
memory: Imagine cupping your hands around your mouth and calling your friend's name across a field. Using your mouth to call someone helps you remember 呼.

sentence.japanese: 先生が私を呼びました。
sentence.reading: せんせい が わたし を よびました。

fact: 互 is used in words like 互い (each other), 相互 (mutual), and 互換 (compatible). It expresses two or more sides interacting equally.
memory: Imagine two friends shaking hands at the exact same time. They help each other equally—that's 互.

sentence.japanese: 私たちは互いに助け合いました。
sentence.reading: わたしたち は たがい に たすけあいました。

kunyomi: まじわる (majiwaru), まざる (mazaru), まじる (majiru), かう (kau), かわす (kawasu)
fact: 交 appears in many common words such as 交通 (traffic), 交換 (exchange), and 交流 (interaction). The central idea is things crossing or connecting.
memory: Imagine two busy roads crossing at an intersection where people exchange greetings. Crossing and exchanging both point to 交.

sentence.japanese: 電話番号を交換しました。
sentence.reading: でんわばんごう を こうかん しました。

fact: 御 is one of the most common honorific kanji in Japanese. It appears as the polite prefixes お and ご in words like お茶 (tea), ご飯 (meal), and 御手洗 (toilet).
memory: Imagine bowing respectfully before presenting a gift. That extra respect added to everyday words reminds you of 御.

sentence.japanese: ご飯を食べましょう。
sentence.reading: ごはん を たべましょう。

kunyomi: あやまる (ayamaru), あやまち (ayamachi)
fact: 誤 is found in words such as 誤解 (misunderstanding), 誤字 (typo), and 誤答 (wrong answer). It always relates to mistakes or incorrect information.
memory: Imagine sending a text to the wrong person because of a typo. That embarrassing mistake reminds you of 誤.

sentence.japanese: その情報は誤っています。
sentence.reading: その じょうほう は あやまって います。

fact: 候 is commonly used in words like 天候 (weather), 気候 (climate), and 候補 (candidate). In old Japanese letters, 候う (そうろう) was a polite verb meaning 'to be.'
memory: Imagine looking up at the sky for signs of changing weather before leaving home. Those signs of the climate remind you of 候.

sentence.japanese: 今日は天候が不安定です。
sentence.reading: きょう は てんこう が ふあんてい です。
sentence.romaji: kyou wa tenkou ga fuantei desu

kunyomi: むく (muku), むける (mukeru), むかう (mukau), むこう (mukou)
fact: 向 appears in many useful words such as 方向 (direction), 傾向 (tendency), and 向かう (to head toward).
memory: Imagine a compass pointing toward your destination. Wherever it faces is the direction represented by 向.

sentence.japanese: 駅へ向かっています。
sentence.reading: えき へ むかって います。
sentence.english: I am heading to the station.

kunyomi: さいわい (saiwai), しあわせ (shiawase)
fact: 幸 appears in words like 幸福 (happiness), 不幸 (misfortune), and 幸せ (happiness). It is one of the most positive kanji in Japanese.
memory: Imagine receiving wonderful news that fills your heart with happiness. That joyful feeling reminds you of 幸.

sentence.japanese: 幸せな毎日を過ごしています。
sentence.reading: しあわせな まいにち を すごして います。

kunyomi: さら (sara), ふける (fukeru), ふかす (fukasu)
fact: 更 appears in everyday words such as 変更 (change), 更新 (update), and 更衣室 (changing room). It represents changing or renewing something.
memory: Imagine replacing an old sign with a brand-new one. Changing the old into something new reminds you of 更.

sentence.japanese: 予定を変更しました。
sentence.reading: よてい を へんこう しました。

fact: 構 is found in important words such as 構造 (structure), 機構 (mechanism), and 結構 (splendid; 'that's enough'). Although 結構 originally meant 'well-constructed,' today it often means 'fine' or 'no thank you.'
memory: Imagine workers carefully building the framework of a house. That sturdy structure reminds you of 構.

sentence.japanese: 新しい家を構えました。
sentence.reading: あたらしい いえ を かまえました。

fact: 港 appears in famous place names such as 横浜港 (Port of Yokohama) and 神戸港 (Port of Kobe). Since Japan is an island nation, ports have played a vital role in trade and culture for centuries.
memory: Imagine dozens of ships gathering at a calm harbor before sailing across the ocean. That busy waterfront is 港.

sentence.japanese: 港には多くの船があります。
sentence.reading: みなと には おおく の ふね が あります。
sentence.romaji: minato ni wa ooku no fune ga arimasu
sentence.english: There are many ships at the harbor.

kunyomi: おりる (oriru), おろす (orosu), ふる (furu)
fact: 降 is commonly used in weather and transportation. It appears in 雨が降る (rain falls), 降りる (to get off), and 以降 (after, since).
memory: Imagine rain falling from the sky while passengers get off a train. Both things move downward, helping you remember 降.

sentence.japanese: 駅で電車を降りました。
sentence.reading: えき で でんしゃ を おりました。
sentence.english: I got off the train at the station.

fact: 号 is used in addresses, train names, room numbers, and editions of newspapers. You'll often see it in 番号 (number), 信号 (traffic signal), and 一号 (No. 1).
memory: Imagine every house, train, and room having its own number plate. Those identifying numbers remind you of 号.

sentence.japanese: 電話番号を書いてください。
sentence.reading: でんわばんごう を かいて ください。

fact: In ancient Japan, a 刻 was a unit of time. Today the kanji appears in 時刻 (time), 深刻 (serious), and 彫刻 (sculpture).
memory: Imagine carving marks into a wooden stick to count the passing hours. Every carved mark reminds you of 刻.

sentence.japanese: 出発時刻を確認してください。
sentence.reading: しゅっぱつ じこく を かくにん して ください。
sentence.romaji: shuppatsu jikoku o kakunin shite kudasai

fact: 告 appears in many common words such as 広告 (advertisement), 報告 (report), and 告白 (confession). The basic meaning is to tell or announce something.
memory: Imagine standing on a stage with a microphone, making an important announcement to everyone. That act of telling people reminds you of 告.

sentence.japanese: 先生に結果を報告しました。
sentence.reading: せんせい に けっか を ほうこく しました。
sentence.romaji: sensei ni kekka o houkoku shimashita
sentence.english: I reported the results to the teacher.

fact: 込 is very common in everyday Japanese. It appears in 混みます (to become crowded), 申し込む (to apply), and 振り込む (to transfer money). The core idea is 'going into' or 'being packed inside.'
memory: Imagine people squeezing into an already full train. Everyone keeps going in until it's completely crowded. That's 込.

sentence.japanese: 朝の電車はとても込みます。
sentence.reading: あさ の でんしゃ は とても こみます。
sentence.romaji: asa no densha wa totemo komimasu
sentence.english: The morning train gets very crowded.

fact: 困 is commonly used in phrases like 困る (to be troubled), 困難 (difficulty), and 貧困 (poverty). It expresses being stuck in a difficult situation.
memory: Imagine being trapped inside a box with no way out. Feeling stuck and troubled helps you remember 困.

sentence.japanese: お金がなくて困っています。
sentence.reading: おかね が なくて こまって います。
sentence.english: I'm having trouble because I have no money.

fact: 婚 appears in many family-related words such as 結婚 (marriage), 婚約 (engagement), and 新婚 (newly married).
memory: Imagine a couple exchanging wedding rings and beginning a new life together. That happy wedding ceremony reminds you of 婚.

sentence.japanese: 姉は去年結婚しました。
sentence.reading: あね は きょねん けっこん しました。
sentence.english: My older sister got married last year.

fact: 差 is used in many everyday words such as 差別 (discrimination), 時差 (time difference), and 差額 (price difference). It represents the idea of a gap or difference.
memory: Imagine placing two rulers side by side and noticing one is longer than the other. That gap between them is 差.

sentence.japanese: 日本とインドには時差があります。
sentence.reading: にほん と インド には じさ が あります。
sentence.romaji: Nihon to Indo ni wa jisa ga arimasu
sentence.english: There is a time difference between Japan and India.

fact: 座 appears in many common words such as 座席 (seat), 星座 (constellation), and 講座 (course or lecture). Historically, 座 also referred to groups of merchants and performers in Japan.
memory: Imagine finding an empty seat in a crowded train and finally sitting down with relief. That comfortable seat reminds you of 座.

sentence.japanese: ここに座ってください。
sentence.reading: ここ に すわって ください。

fact: One of the most useful Japanese grammar patterns is 「一番 (ichiban)」 and 「最も (mottomo)」, both meaning 'the most.' You'll see 最 everywhere—from weather forecasts like 最高気温 (highest temperature) to reviews saying 最高! ('Awesome!').
memory: Imagine the SUN (日) shining on the EAR (耳) of your FRIEND (又), making them hear the 'BEST' news ever. Everything under this kanji becomes the MOST.

sentence.japanese: 今日は 最後 の 授業です。
sentence.reading: きょうは さいご の じゅぎょうです。
sentence.romaji: kyou wa saigo no jugyou desu

fact: Although 歳 is the official kanji for age, Japanese signs, forms, and advertisements often use the simpler 才 instead because it's easier and faster to write.
memory: Think of a tiny sprout growing from a tree branch. That small shoot represents natural TALENT beginning to grow.

sentence.japanese: 妹は 十才 です。
sentence.reading: いもうとは じっさい です。
sentence.english: My younger sister is ten years old.

fact: In Japan, birthdays are celebrated much like in the West today, but historically everyone became one year older together on New Year's Day. The kanji 歳 has been connected with counting age for centuries.
memory: Think of this as the 'upgraded' version of 才. It has extra parts because getting older means gaining more years and more experiences.

sentence.japanese: 父は 五十歳 です。
sentence.reading: ちちは ごじゅっさい です。
sentence.english: My father is fifty years old.

fact: In Japan you'll often see the stamp or label 「済」 on documents. It simply means 'completed' or 'processed,' showing the task is finished.
memory: Water (氵) washes everything clean once the work is FINISHED. Imagine washing your hands after completing a big project.

sentence.japanese: 宿題は もう 済みました。
sentence.reading: しゅくだいは もう すみました。
sentence.english: The homework has already been finished.

fact: You'll often hear 「〜の際に」 in announcements and formal Japanese. It politely means 'on the occasion of' or 'when,' making this kanji very common in business and public notices.
memory: Imagine standing at the EDGE of a cliff beside a HILL (阝). That dangerous boundary reminds you of the border or occasion where something happens.

sentence.japanese: 日本へ 行く 際に パスポートが 必要です。
sentence.reading: にほんへ いく さいに パスポートが ひつようです。
sentence.romaji: nihon e iku sai ni pasupooto ga hitsuyou desu
sentence.english: You need a passport when going to Japan.

fact: You'll often see 在 in words like 現在 (now), 在学 (currently enrolled), and 在住 (currently living). It is commonly used to describe where someone or something exists.
memory: Imagine a person standing firmly on the EARTH (土). Wherever they're standing is where they EXIST or are LOCATED.

sentence.japanese: 私は 東京に 在住しています。
sentence.reading: わたしは とうきょうに ざいじゅうしています。
sentence.romaji: watashi wa toukyou ni zaijuu shiteimasu

fact: The word 犯罪 (crime) appears frequently in Japanese news. The kanji 罪 is also used in moral or religious contexts to mean 'sin' or 'guilt.'
memory: Imagine someone trapped inside a NET (罒) after breaking the law. Being caught reminds you of CRIME and GUILT.

sentence.japanese: 彼は 無罪でした。
sentence.reading: かれは むざいでした。

fact: Japan's Ministry of Finance is called 財務省 (Zaimu-shō). The kanji 財 appears in many business, banking, and economic terms.
memory: Imagine a valuable SHELL (貝), once used as money, beside useful TOOLS. Together they represent WEALTH and PROPERTY.

sentence.japanese: 財布を 家に 忘れました。
sentence.reading: さいふを いえに わすれました。
sentence.english: I forgot my wallet at home.

fact: The kanji 昨 is rarely used by itself but appears in common words like 昨日 (yesterday), 昨年 (last year), and 昨夜 (last night).
memory: Think of the SUN (日) moving behind yesterday's horizon. Once the sun has passed, it becomes YESTERDAY.

sentence.japanese: 昨日は 雨でした。
sentence.reading: きのうは あめでした。

fact: The word 警察 (police) literally combines 'police' (警) with 'inspect/observe' (察), reflecting the role of watching over public safety.
memory: Imagine carefully examining every corner of a HOUSE (宀) before making a decision. Careful observation leads to understanding.

sentence.japanese: 警察が 事故を 調べています。
sentence.reading: けいさつが じこを しらべています。
sentence.english: The police are investigating the accident.

fact: Although 殺 literally means 'to kill,' it is often used figuratively in everyday Japanese. For example, 時間を殺す (jikan o korosu) means 'to kill time,' just like the English expression.
memory: Imagine using a weapon to stop a moving target. The action completely ends its movement, helping you remember the meaning 'to kill.'

sentence.japanese: 駅で 時間を 殺しました。
sentence.reading: えきで じかんを ころしました。
sentence.english: I killed time at the station.

fact: The popular Japanese word 雑誌 (zasshi) means 'magazine.' Originally, magazines contained a 'mixture' of articles, perfectly matching the meaning of 雑.
memory: Picture many different birds gathering on one tree, creating a noisy MIX of sounds and colors. That's 雑—many things mixed together.

sentence.japanese: この 本は 内容が 複雑です。
sentence.reading: この ほんは ないようが ふくざつです。
sentence.romaji: kono hon wa naiyou ga fukuzatsu desu

fact: 参 appears in many useful words like 参加 (participation) and 参拝 (visiting a shrine). During New Year, millions of Japanese people perform 初詣 by visiting shrines to pray for good fortune.
memory: Imagine three people walking together toward a shrine. They are PARTICIPATING in a festival by GOING there together.

sentence.japanese: 私は 会議に 参加します。
sentence.reading: わたしは かいぎに さんかします。
sentence.romaji: watashi wa kaigi ni sanka shimasu
sentence.english: I will participate in the meeting.

fact: Cherry blossom season is famous for 散る (chiru), when pink petals scatter through the air. Many Japanese people consider this brief beauty a symbol of life's impermanence.
memory: Imagine flower petals being blown in every direction by the wind. They SCATTER everywhere, creating a beautiful spring scene.

sentence.japanese: 桜の 花が 散っています。
sentence.reading: さくらの はなが ちっています。

fact: On Japanese websites and social media, 賛成 (agreement) is commonly used during polls and discussions. If you agree with an opinion, you can simply say 「賛成です！」 ('I agree!').
memory: Imagine someone giving valuable treasure (貝) as a reward because they completely APPROVE of your work. Praise comes with support.

sentence.japanese: 私は この 意見に 賛成です。
sentence.reading: わたしは この いけんに さんせいです。
sentence.romaji: watashi wa kono iken ni sansei desu

fact: The phrase 残念 (zannen), meaning 'regrettable' or 'what a pity,' is one of the most common expressions in Japanese. Originally, it referred to something 'remaining' in one's mind after disappointment.
memory: Imagine finishing dinner but leaving a few pieces of food on the plate. Those LEFTOVERS REMAIN after everyone else has finished.

sentence.japanese: ご飯を 残さないで ください。
sentence.reading: ごはんを のこさないで ください。

fact: Many respected professions in Japan end with 師, such as 医師 (doctor), 教師 (teacher), and 美容師 (hairdresser), emphasizing skilled professionals.
memory: Imagine a MASTER standing proudly in front of many students, sharing years of wisdom and experience.

sentence.japanese: 私の 父は 医師です。
sentence.reading: わたしの ちちは いしです。
sentence.romaji: watashi no chichi wa ishi desu

fact: Japanese people often avoid pointing directly at others with a finger because it can be considered impolite. Instead, they may gesture with an open hand.
memory: Picture a HAND (扌) stretching out one FINGER to POINT at a destination. The hand radical makes the meaning easy to remember.

sentence.japanese: 先生が 黒板を 指しました。
sentence.reading: せんせいが こくばんを さしました。
sentence.english: The teacher pointed at the blackboard.

fact: 支 appears in many important words like 支店 (branch office), 支払い (payment), and 支える (to support), making it a highly practical kanji in daily life and business.
memory: Imagine a strong hand HOLDING UP a heavy tree branch so it doesn't fall. That's SUPPORT in action.

sentence.japanese: 家族が 私を 支えてくれます。
sentence.reading: かぞくが わたしを ささえてくれます。
sentence.romaji: kazoku ga watashi o sasaete kuremasu

fact: The modern Japanese word 投資 (tōshi), meaning 'investment,' is built using this kanji. It's one of the first kanji you'll encounter when reading business or financial news.
memory: Imagine piling valuable SHELLS (貝), once used as money, into a treasure chest. Those resources become your CAPITAL.

sentence.japanese: 彼は 株に 投資しています。
sentence.reading: かれは かぶに とうししています。
sentence.romaji: kare wa kabu ni toushi shiteimasu

fact: Japan celebrates 「いい歯の日」 (Good Teeth Day) every year on November 8 because 11 (ii = good) and 8 (ha = tooth) create a memorable wordplay encouraging dental health.
memory: Imagine opening your mouth to reveal a row of sharp TEETH lined up like tiny white fence posts.

sentence.japanese: 毎日 歯を 磨きます。
sentence.reading: まいにち はを みがきます。
sentence.english: I brush my teeth every day.

fact: Japanese people often say 「似ていますね！」 ('You look alike!') when siblings or family members resemble each other. It's a common compliment in everyday conversation.
memory: Imagine TWO PEOPLE standing side by side looking almost identical. They RESEMBLE each other so closely that it's hard to tell them apart.

sentence.japanese: 妹は 母に よく 似ています。
sentence.reading: いもうとは ははに よく にています。
sentence.romaji: imouto wa haha ni yoku niteimasu
sentence.english: My younger sister looks a lot like my mother.

fact: At Japanese train stations, you'll constantly hear 「次は〜です」 ('Next is...'), making 次 one of the most frequently seen kanji while traveling.
memory: Imagine standing in a LINE waiting for your TURN. The NEXT person steps forward as soon as the previous one finishes.

sentence.japanese: 次の 電車を 待ちます。
sentence.reading: つぎの でんしゃを まちます。
sentence.english: I'll wait for the next train.

kunyomi: なお.す (nao-su), なお.る (nao-ru), おさ.める (osa-meru), おさ.まる (osa-maru)
fact: The famous Meiji era (明治時代) includes this kanji. 治 can mean both 'to govern' a country and 'to heal' a disease, expressing the idea of restoring order.
memory: Imagine WATER (氵) washing away illness until the body returns to order. Whether healing a person or governing a country, everything becomes well again.

sentence.japanese: 風邪は もう 治りました。
sentence.reading: かぜは もう なおりました。
sentence.english: My cold has already gotten better.

fact: The radical 示 originally represented an altar for offerings to the gods. Today it appears in many kanji related to religion, signs, and showing information.
memory: Imagine a SIGN standing in front of you, clearly SHOWING the correct direction. It points the way for everyone.

sentence.japanese: 先生は 答えを 示しました。
sentence.reading: せんせいは こたえを しめしました。

fact: The word 辞書 (jisho), meaning 'dictionary,' literally combines 'words' (辞) and 'book' (書). Another common word, 辞める, means 'to quit' or 'to resign,' making this kanji useful both in study and work.
memory: Imagine giving a farewell SPEECH filled with WORDS before RESIGNING from your job. Your final words help you remember 辞.

sentence.japanese: 彼は 会社を 辞めました。
sentence.reading: かれは かいしゃを やめました。

fact: Japanese uses 式 for many important events, such as 卒業式 (graduation ceremony), 入学式 (entrance ceremony), and 結婚式 (wedding ceremony).
memory: Imagine everyone wearing matching clothes and following the same STYLE during a CEREMONY. Everything follows one fixed FORM.

sentence.japanese: 明日は 卒業式です。
sentence.reading: あしたは そつぎょうしきです。

fact: The word 知識 (chishiki), meaning 'knowledge,' is one of the most common compounds using this kanji. It appears frequently in education, books, and exams.
memory: Imagine using your WORDS (言) to recognize the difference between many things. Careful observation becomes KNOWLEDGE.

sentence.japanese: 日本の 歴史について 知識があります。
sentence.reading: にほんの れきしについて ちしきがあります。
sentence.romaji: nihon no rekishi ni tsuite chishiki ga arimasu
sentence.english: I have knowledge about Japanese history.

fact: The common word 失礼します (Shitsurei shimasu), meaning 'Excuse me,' literally contains the kanji for 'mistake' or 'loss of manners,' reflecting Japanese politeness.
memory: Imagine accidentally DROPPING something important from your hands. Once it's gone, you've LOST it.

sentence.japanese: 失敗しても 大丈夫です。
sentence.reading: しっぱいしても だいじょうぶです。
sentence.english: It's okay even if you fail.

fact: The famous phrase 実は (jitsu wa...) means 'Actually...' and is commonly used before revealing surprising information. The kanji also means 'fruit' or 'something that has borne results.'
memory: Imagine a tree FULL OF FRUIT. The fruit is the REAL RESULT of all the tree's hard work, helping you remember both 'fruit' and 'reality.'

sentence.japanese: 実は 日本へ 行ったことがあります。
sentence.reading: じつは にほんへ いったことがあります。
sentence.romaji: jitsu wa nihon e itta koto ga arimasu

fact: The famous expression 「若者 (wakamono)」 means 'young people.' In Japan, many companies also celebrate 新入社員 (new employees), who are often called 若手 (wakate), meaning 'young talent.'
memory: Imagine fresh GRASS growing from the ground. Young plants are full of energy, just like YOUNG people beginning their lives.

sentence.japanese: 彼は まだ 若いです。
sentence.reading: かれは まだ わかいです。

fact: One of the first useful verbs learners encounter is 取る ('to take'). It appears in many everyday expressions like 写真を取る ('take a photo') and 予約を取る ('make a reservation').
memory: Imagine reaching out with your HAND to TAKE something from someone. The hand action makes the meaning easy to remember.

sentence.japanese: 写真を 取りました。
sentence.reading: しゃしんを とりました。

fact: The word お守り (omamori) refers to the lucky charms sold at Japanese shrines and temples. People carry them for protection, health, success, or safe travel.
memory: Imagine standing under a ROOF while protecting your family from danger. A safe home reminds you to PROTECT and GUARD.

sentence.japanese: 約束を 守ってください。
sentence.reading: やくそくを まもってください。

fact: The phrase 色々な種類 (iroiro na shurui) means 'various kinds.' The kanji also literally means 'seed,' connecting the idea that many different types grow from different seeds.
memory: Imagine planting different SEEDS in a garden. Each seed grows into a different KIND of plant.

sentence.japanese: この 店には いろいろな 種類が あります。
sentence.reading: この みせには いろいろな しゅるいが あります。
sentence.romaji: kono mise ni wa iroiro na shurui ga arimasu
sentence.english: This store has many different kinds.

fact: Japanese sake is brewed from rice, water, yeast, and kōji mold. It's an important part of festivals, weddings, shrine ceremonies, and celebrations throughout Japan.
memory: Imagine WATER being transformed into a delicious cup of SAKE. The water radical reminds you that all alcoholic drinks are liquids.

sentence.japanese: 父は 日本酒が 好きです。
sentence.reading: ちちは にほんしゅが すきです。
sentence.romaji: chichi wa nihonshu ga suki desu

kunyomi: う.ける (u-keru), う.かる (u-karu), う.かれる (u-kareru), う.かがう (u-kagau)
fact: The verb 受ける is one of the most useful in Japanese. You can 受ける a test, a lesson, medical treatment, or even a phone call, making this kanji extremely common in daily life.
memory: Imagine opening both hands to RECEIVE a gift from someone. Open hands are always ready to ACCEPT something.

sentence.japanese: 来週 試験を 受けます。
sentence.reading: らいしゅう しけんを うけます。
sentence.english: I will take an exam next week.

kunyomi: おさ.める (osa-meru), おさ.まる (osa-maru)
fact: The kanji 収 appears in useful words like 収入 (income), 回収 (collection), and 収集 (gathering). It often carries the idea of bringing things together.
memory: Imagine gathering coins into a treasure chest. You COLLECT them one by one until they become your INCOME.

sentence.japanese: 毎月の 収入は 十万円です。
sentence.reading: まいつきの しゅうにゅうは じゅうまんえんです。
sentence.romaji: maitsuki no shuunyuu wa juuman en desu

kunyomi: やど (yado), やど.る (yado-ru), やど.す (yado-su)
fact: Traditional Japanese inns are called 旅館 (ryokan), but the kanji 宿 is often seen in words related to lodging, such as 宿泊 (overnight stay) and 民宿 (family-run guesthouse).
memory: Imagine sleeping safely UNDER A ROOF after a long journey. That building is your LODGE for the night.

sentence.japanese: 京都で 一泊 宿泊しました。
sentence.reading: きょうとで いっぱく しゅくはくしました。
sentence.english: I stayed one night in Kyoto.

fact: The ending ～術 appears in famous Japanese martial arts like 柔術 (Jujutsu) and 剣術 (Kenjutsu). It refers to a mastered technique or specialized skill.
memory: Imagine a martial artist practicing the same move thousands of times until it becomes a perfect TECHNIQUE.

sentence.japanese: 日本の 技術は 世界的に 有名です。
sentence.reading: にほんの ぎじゅつは せかいてきに ゆうめいです。
sentence.romaji: nihon no gijutsu wa sekaiteki ni yuumei desu

fact: You'll often encounter 処 in signs such as ごみ処理場 (garbage processing plant) and in computer messages like 処理中 ('Processing...').
memory: Imagine a worker sorting packages into the correct PLACE. Each item is carefully PROCESSED before moving on.

sentence.japanese: この 問題を 早く 処理しましょう。
sentence.reading: この もんだいを はやく しょりしましょう。
sentence.romaji: kono mondai o hayaku shori shimashou
sentence.english: Let's deal with this problem quickly.

kunyomi: はじ.め (haji-me), はじ.めて (hajimete), はつ (hatsu), うい (ui)
fact: Japan has many traditions beginning with 初 (first), such as 初日の出 (first sunrise of the year), 初夢 (first dream), and 初詣 (the first shrine visit of the New Year).
memory: Imagine taking your VERY FIRST step while wearing a brand-new kimono. That exciting beginning helps you remember 初.

sentence.japanese: 私は 初めて 日本へ 行きました。
sentence.reading: わたしは はじめて にほんへ いきました。
sentence.romaji: watashi wa hajimete Nihon e ikimashita
sentence.english: I went to Japan for the first time.

fact: The common word 一緒 (issho), meaning 'together,' literally comes from the idea of several threads tied into one cord.
memory: Imagine tying many THREADS together into one strong rope. That rope marks the BEGINNING of working TOGETHER.

sentence.japanese: 一緒に 勉強しましょう。
sentence.reading: いっしょに べんきょうしましょう。

kunyomi: たす.ける (tasu-keru), たす.かる (tasu-karu), すけ (suke)
fact: The phrase 助けて! ('Help!') is one of the first emergency expressions many Japanese learners memorize. The kanji also appears in words like 援助 (support) and 補助 (assistance).
memory: Imagine reaching out your HAND to pull someone up after they fall. That's exactly what HELP looks like.

sentence.japanese: 友達が 私を 助けてくれました。
sentence.reading: ともだちが わたしを たすけてくれました。
sentence.romaji: tomodachi ga watashi o tasukete kuremashita

fact: Every winter, many Japanese homes perform 大掃除 (ōsōji), a major year-end cleaning. The kanji 除 appears in 掃除 (cleaning), literally meaning 'to remove dirt.'
memory: Imagine sweeping away piles of leaves until the path is completely CLEAR. You REMOVE everything that doesn't belong.

sentence.japanese: 部屋を 掃除しました。
sentence.reading: へやを そうじしました。

fact: During sports events in Japan, you'll often hear chants like 『頑張れ！勝て！』 ('Do your best! Win!'). The kanji 勝 is common in sports, games, and competitions.
memory: Imagine raising a championship trophy high above your head after defeating every opponent. That's the feeling of VICTORY.

sentence.japanese: 私たちの チームが 勝ちました。
sentence.reading: わたしたちの チームが かちました。

fact: The famous shopping districts of Japan, such as 商店街 (shotengai), are long streets filled with small family-run businesses, restaurants, and local shops.
memory: Imagine a busy MARKET where merchants are constantly buying and selling goods. That's the world of BUSINESS and COMMERCE.

sentence.japanese: 駅の近くに 大きい 商店街が あります。
sentence.reading: えきの ちかくに おおきい しょうてんがいが あります。
sentence.romaji: eki no chikaku ni ookii shotengai ga arimasu
sentence.english: There is a large shopping street near the station.

fact: The famous lucky cat, 招き猫 (Maneki-neko), literally means 'beckoning cat.' The raised paw is believed to invite customers and good fortune into shops.
memory: Picture someone waving their HAND toward you, inviting you to come closer. That welcoming gesture means INVITE.

sentence.japanese: 友達を 家に 招きました。
sentence.reading: ともだちを いえに まねきました。
sentence.english: I invited my friend to my house.

fact: You'll hear 消してください ('Please turn it off') everywhere in Japan, whether it's lights, televisions, or mobile phones. This kanji is essential in everyday life.
memory: Imagine pouring WATER onto a burning candle until the flame DISAPPEARS. Water helps EXTINGUISH the fire.

sentence.japanese: 電気を 消してください。
sentence.reading: でんきを けしてください。

fact: In Japanese internet slang, people often type 「笑」 after a sentence to show laughter, much like 'LOL.' It is also shortened to a single 「w」 because 笑 is read as warau.
memory: Imagine a smiling face with eyes closed from laughing so hard. That joyful expression helps you remember LAUGH.

sentence.japanese: 彼は 楽しそうに 笑っています。
sentence.reading: かれは たのしそうに わらっています。

fact: Traditional Japanese shrines often use paper streamers called 垂 (shide), which hang from sacred ropes to mark holy places and ward off evil spirits.
memory: Imagine long tree branches HANGING DOWN toward the ground after heavy rain. Their drooping shape matches the kanji's meaning.

sentence.japanese: 木の 枝が 地面まで 垂れています。
sentence.reading: きの えだが じめんまで たれています。
sentence.romaji: ki no eda ga jimen made tareteimasu
sentence.english: The tree branches are hanging down to the ground.

fact: The word 日常 (nichijou), meaning 'everyday life,' is extremely common in Japanese. You'll also hear 非常口 (emergency exit), where 非常 literally means 'not normal' or 'emergency.'
memory: Imagine following the SAME DAILY ROUTINE every morning. Because nothing changes, it becomes your NORMAL habit.

sentence.japanese: 今日は 通常通り 営業します。
sentence.reading: きょうは つうじょうどおり えいぎょうします。
sentence.romaji: kyou wa tsuujou doori eigyou shimasu
sentence.english: We are open as usual today.

fact: The modern word 情報 (jōhō), meaning 'information,' is one of the most common technology-related words in Japanese. The same kanji also appears in 感情 (emotion), showing how it covers both feelings and information.
memory: Imagine your HEART filled with many FEELINGS. Everything you feel becomes an EMOTION stored inside your heart.

sentence.japanese: 新しい 情報を 教えてください。
sentence.reading: あたらしい じょうほうを おしえてください。
sentence.english: Please tell me the new information.

fact: You'll frequently encounter 状況 (jōkyō), meaning 'situation,' in Japanese news reports. Another common word, 招待状, means 'invitation card,' showing that 状 can also refer to a written document.
memory: Imagine checking a STATUS SCREEN that shows the CONDITION of every system. It tells you exactly what STATE everything is in.

sentence.japanese: 状況を 説明してください。
sentence.reading: じょうきょうを せつめいしてください。

fact: Many Japanese job titles use 職, including 転職 (changing jobs), 就職 (getting employed), and 職場 (workplace). It's one of the most important business kanji.
memory: Imagine putting on your WORK UNIFORM before starting your profession. Wearing it reminds you of your JOB.

sentence.japanese: 兄は 新しい 職場で 働いています。
sentence.reading: あには あたらしい しょくばで はたらいています。
sentence.romaji: ani wa atarashii shokuba de hataraiteimasu
sentence.english: My older brother works at a new workplace.

fact: The word 信号 (shingō), meaning 'traffic signal,' literally means 'trust signal.' Drivers trust the signal to safely guide traffic every day.
memory: Imagine someone keeping their WORD to another PERSON. When words can be trusted, they create BELIEF and TRUST.

sentence.japanese: 私は あなたを 信じます。
sentence.reading: わたしは あなたを しんじます。

fact: Japanese people often say 「おやすみなさい」 before going to bed. The kanji 寝 appears in words like 寝室 (bedroom) and 寝坊 (oversleeping), making it one of the most practical daily-life kanji.
memory: Imagine crawling under a ROOF into a warm bed. Once you're safely inside, you immediately fall asleep.

sentence.japanese: 毎日 十時に 寝ます。
sentence.reading: まいにち じゅうじに ねます。
sentence.english: I go to bed at ten every day.

kunyomi: ふか-い (fuka-i), ふか-める (fuka-meru), ふか-まる (fuka-maru)
fact: Japan is home to some of the world's deepest hot springs. The kanji 深 also appears in 深夜 (late at night) and 深刻 (serious), extending its meaning beyond physical depth.
memory: Imagine diving into DEEP WATER until you can no longer see the surface. The deeper you go, the darker everything becomes.

sentence.japanese: この 川は とても 深いです。
sentence.reading: この かわは とても ふかいです。
sentence.romaji: kono kawa wa totemo fukai desu

fact: 申 is widely used in polite Japanese. Expressions like 申し訳ありません ('I'm very sorry') and 申します ('My name is...') are essential in formal conversations.
memory: Imagine respectfully raising your hand to SPEAK before making an important REPORT to your boss.

sentence.japanese: 私は 田中と 申します。
sentence.reading: わたしは たなかと もうします。

fact: In Shinto belief, Japan is said to have 八百万の神 (yaoyorozu no kami)—'eight million gods,' meaning countless spirits believed to live in mountains, rivers, trees, and nature.
memory: Imagine a sacred SHRINE where a DIVINE SPIRIT quietly watches over visitors. The altar reminds you of a GOD.

sentence.japanese: 神社で お祈りしました。
sentence.reading: じんじゃで おいのりしました。
sentence.english: I prayed at a Shinto shrine.

fact: Every spring, Japanese weather reports announce 春一番 (Haru Ichiban), the first strong wind of spring. The verb 吹く is used to describe the wind blowing across the country.
memory: Imagine taking a deep BREATH and BLOWING out birthday candles with one big puff of air.

sentence.japanese: 今日は 強い 風が 吹いています。
sentence.reading: きょうは つよい かぜが ふいています。
sentence.romaji: kyou wa tsuyoi kaze ga fuiteimasu
sentence.english: A strong wind is blowing today.

fact: The kanji 数 appears in countless everyday words like 数学 (mathematics), 人数 (number of people), and 数字 (numeral). It's one of the most frequently used kanji in Japanese.
memory: Imagine counting piles of rice grains one by one. The more you count, the larger the NUMBER becomes.

sentence.japanese: 学生の 数を 数えてください。
sentence.reading: がくせいの かずを かぞえてください。
sentence.romaji: gakusei no kazu o kazoete kudasai
sentence.english: Please count the number of students.

fact: Japan has many words ending in 制, such as 税制 (tax system), 制度 (system), and 予約制 (reservation-only). The kanji often refers to organized rules or systems.
memory: Imagine a ruler creating RULES that CONTROL how everyone behaves. Those rules form a SYSTEM.

sentence.japanese: この 図書館は 会員制です。
sentence.reading: この としょかんは かいいんせいです。

fact: The kanji 性 appears in many abstract words such as 可能性 (possibility), 安全性 (safety), and 性格 (personality). It often expresses a person's or object's inherent nature.
memory: Imagine a HEART revealing someone's true CHARACTER. Deep inside is their real NATURE.

sentence.japanese: 彼は 明るい 性格です。
sentence.reading: かれは あかるい せいかくです。

fact: The expression 成功 (success) is one of the most encouraging words in Japanese. The kanji 成 carries the idea of something becoming complete or successfully achieved.
memory: Imagine climbing a mountain until you finally reach the summit. You have ACHIEVED your goal and BECOME successful.

sentence.japanese: 夢が 現実に なりました。
sentence.reading: ゆめが げんじつに なりました。

fact: The word 政府 (government) appears almost daily in Japanese newspapers and television news. The kanji 政 is central to discussions about politics and public administration.
memory: Imagine a GOVERNMENT leader making important decisions that guide the entire country. Those decisions represent POLITICS.

sentence.japanese: 政府は 新しい 政策を 発表しました。
sentence.reading: せいふは あたらしい せいさくを はっぴょうしました。
sentence.romaji: seifu wa atarashii seisaku o happyou shimashita
sentence.english: The government announced a new policy.

kunyomi: は-れる (ha-reru), は-らす (ha-rasu), は-れ (ha-re)
fact: Japanese weather forecasts frequently use 快晴 (kaisei), meaning 'perfectly clear sky.' During hanami (cherry blossom viewing), everyone hopes for a 晴れの日—a beautiful sunny day.
memory: Imagine the SUN shining brightly after dark clouds disappear. The sky becomes completely CLEAR and SUNNY.

sentence.japanese: 今日は よく 晴れています。
sentence.reading: きょうは よく はれています。

fact: The word 精神 (seishin), meaning 'mind' or 'spirit,' is widely used in Japanese. Another common word, 精一杯 (seiippai), means 'doing one's very best.'
memory: Imagine polishing a crystal until it becomes perfectly PURE and REFINED. That shining energy represents your SPIRIT.

sentence.japanese: 精一杯 頑張ります。
sentence.reading: せいいっぱい がんばります。

kunyomi: しず-か (shizu-ka), しず-まる (shizu-maru), しず-める (shizu-meru)
fact: Japanese trains are famously quiet. Signs often ask passengers to keep the carriage 静か (shizuka), reflecting the country's emphasis on respecting shared spaces.
memory: Imagine a peaceful lake with not a single ripple. Everything is perfectly STILL and QUIET.

sentence.japanese: この 図書館は とても 静かです。
sentence.reading: この としょかんは とても しずかです。
sentence.romaji: kono toshokan wa totemo shizuka desu

fact: In Japanese restaurants, staff often ask 「何名様ですか？」 before showing you to your 席 (seat). Reserved seats on trains are called 指定席 (shiteiseki).
memory: Imagine finding your RESERVED SEAT in a crowded theater before the show begins.

sentence.japanese: ここに 座っても いいですか。
sentence.reading: ここに すわっても いいですか。
sentence.romaji: koko ni suwatte mo ii desu ka

fact: Many Japanese folktales begin with the famous phrase 「昔々、あるところに…」 ('Long, long ago, in a certain place...'), just like 'Once upon a time' in English.
memory: Imagine opening an old storybook filled with tales from LONG AGO. Every page takes you into the PAST.

sentence.japanese: 昔 この町に 住んでいました。
sentence.reading: むかし このまちに すんでいました。
sentence.english: I used to live in this town long ago.

onyomi: セキ (seki), シャク (shaku), コク (koku)
fact: Japan's famous Zen rock gardens (枯山水, karesansui) use carefully placed stones to represent mountains and islands. A few simple rocks can symbolize an entire landscape.
memory: Imagine picking up a heavy STONE from the ground. It's solid, hard, and impossible to mistake for anything else.

sentence.japanese: 子どもが 川で 石を 拾いました。
sentence.reading: こどもが かわで いしを ひろいました。
sentence.romaji: kodomo ga kawa de ishi o hiroimashita
sentence.english: The child picked up a stone by the river.

fact: The phrase 経験を積む (keiken o tsumu), meaning 'to gain experience,' is very common in Japanese. Experience is imagined as something that gradually piles up over time.
memory: Imagine stacking one box on top of another until they form a huge PILE. Every box ADDS to the total.

sentence.japanese: 雪が 道に 積もっています。
sentence.reading: ゆきが みちに つもっています。
sentence.english: Snow has piled up on the road.

fact: The word 責任 (sekinin), meaning 'responsibility,' is one of the most important business words in Japanese. Taking responsibility is highly valued in Japanese workplace culture.
memory: Imagine carrying a HEAVY BACKPACK full of duties. That weight reminds you of your RESPONSIBILITIES.

sentence.japanese: これは 私の 責任です。
sentence.reading: これは わたしの せきにんです。
sentence.romaji: kore wa watashi no sekinin desu

kunyomi: お-る (o-ru), お-れる (o-reru), おり (ori)
fact: The traditional art of 折り紙 (origami) literally means 'folded paper.' It has become one of Japan's most famous cultural exports.
memory: Imagine carefully FOLDING a sheet of paper into a beautiful crane. One wrong move and the paper could tear or BREAK.

sentence.japanese: 紙を 半分に 折ってください。
sentence.reading: かみを はんぶんに おってください。
sentence.romaji: kami o hanbun ni otte kudasai
sentence.english: Please fold the paper in half.

fact: Northern Japan is famous for heavy snowfall. Cities like Sapporo celebrate giant snow festivals where enormous sculptures are carved entirely from snow.
memory: Imagine soft SNOW falling from the sky until the entire ground turns white. The white blanket makes this kanji easy to remember.

sentence.japanese: 昨日は 雪が 降りました。
sentence.reading: きのうは ゆきが ふりました。

kunyomi: た-つ (ta-tsu), た-える (ta-eru), た-やす (ta-yasu)
fact: The word 絶対 (zettai), meaning 'absolutely,' is one of the most frequently used expressions in Japanese. Ironically, the kanji originally meant 'to cut off completely,' giving the idea of something leaving no exceptions.
memory: Imagine cutting a ROPE so completely that the two ends can never be tied together again. The connection is ABSOLUTELY broken.

sentence.japanese: 今日は 絶対に 遅れません。
sentence.reading: きょうは ぜったいに おくれません。
sentence.english: I absolutely won't be late today.

fact: The kanji 戦 appears in many everyday words beyond war, such as 挑戦 (challenge), meaning to 'battle against' a difficult goal. It's commonly used in sports and competitions.
memory: Imagine two armies facing each other on a battlefield. The fierce struggle reminds you of BATTLE.

sentence.japanese: 新しい ことに 挑戦したいです。
sentence.reading: あたらしい ことに ちょうせんしたいです。
sentence.romaji: atarashii koto ni chousen shitai desu
sentence.english: I want to challenge myself with something new.

fact: Japan is an island nation, so ships have played a vital role throughout its history. The famous bullet train may be fast today, but ferries still connect many of Japan's islands.
memory: Imagine a large SHIP floating peacefully across the ocean, carrying passengers from one island to another.

sentence.japanese: 船で 島へ 行きました。
sentence.reading: ふねで しまへ いきました。
sentence.english: I went to the island by ship.

fact: The word 選手 (senshu), meaning 'athlete' or 'player,' literally refers to someone who has been 'selected' to represent a team.
memory: Imagine standing in front of many doors and carefully CHOOSING only one to enter. You SELECT the best option.

sentence.japanese: 好きな 色を 選んでください。
sentence.reading: すきな いろを えらんでください。
sentence.romaji: suki na iro o erande kudasai

fact: The expression 当然 (tōzen), meaning 'of course' or 'naturally,' is extremely common. The kanji 然 often gives words the sense of 'being that way by nature.'
memory: Imagine a campfire burning exactly as expected. Everything happens NATURALLY, just as it should.

sentence.japanese: 自然が とても 美しいです。
sentence.reading: しぜんが とても うつくしいです。

fact: The kanji 全 appears in many common words such as 全部 (everything), 安全 (safety), and 全国 (nationwide). It expresses the idea of completeness or including everything.
memory: Imagine completing a giant puzzle with every single piece in place. Nothing is missing—the WHOLE picture is COMPLETE.

sentence.japanese: 宿題は 全部 終わりました。
sentence.reading: しゅくだいは ぜんぶ おわりました。
sentence.english: I finished all of my homework.

fact: The kanji 祖 appears in words like 祖父 (grandfather) and 祖先 (ancestors). During the Obon festival, many Japanese families honor their ancestors by visiting graves and offering prayers.
memory: Imagine an old FAMILY TREE stretching back through many generations. At the very top stands your ANCESTOR.

sentence.japanese: 祖父は 九十歳です。
sentence.reading: そふは きゅうじゅっさいです。
sentence.english: My grandfather is ninety years old.

fact: Japanese schools often divide students into groups called 組 (kumi), such as 1年2組 ('Year 1, Class 2'). The kanji is also common in words related to teamwork and organizations.
memory: Imagine tying many THREADS together into one strong GROUP. Individual strands become stronger when ORGANIZED together.

sentence.japanese: 私たちは 三人で 組を 作りました。
sentence.reading: わたしたちは さんにんで くみを つくりました。
sentence.romaji: watashitachi wa sannin de kumi o tsukurimashita
sentence.english: The three of us formed a group.

fact: The word 思想 (shisō) means 'thought' or 'philosophy,' while 感想 (kansō) means 'impression.' The kanji 想 often relates to ideas formed in the mind.
memory: Imagine your HEART filled with beautiful IDEAS and IMAGINATION. Thoughts are created inside your heart and mind.

sentence.japanese: 映画の 感想を 教えてください。
sentence.reading: えいがの かんそうを おしえてください。
sentence.romaji: eiga no kansou o oshiete kudasai
sentence.english: Please tell me your thoughts about the movie.

fact: The kanji 争 is used in words like 戦争 (war), 競争 (competition), and 紛争 (conflict). Depending on the context, it can describe anything from sports competitions to international disputes.
memory: Imagine two people pulling on the same rope, each trying to win. Their struggle represents CONFLICT and COMPETITION.

sentence.japanese: 兄と ゲームで 争いました。
sentence.reading: あにと ゲームで あらそいました。
sentence.romaji: ani to geemu de arasoi mashita
sentence.english: I competed with my older brother in a game.

fact: The kanji 相 appears in many everyday words such as 相手 (partner/opponent), 相談 (consultation), and 首相 (prime minister). It often conveys the idea of interacting with another person.
memory: Imagine TWO PEOPLE standing FACE TO FACE, looking at each other. They are PARTNERS interacting MUTUALLY.

sentence.japanese: 困ったら 先生に 相談してください。
sentence.reading: こまったら せんせいに そうだんしてください。
sentence.romaji: komattara sensei ni soudan shite kudasai
sentence.english: If you're in trouble, please consult your teacher.

fact: Many Japanese trains have 窓側 (madogawa) and 通路側 (tsuurogawa) seats. Travelers often request a 窓側 seat to enjoy views of Mount Fuji.
memory: Imagine opening a WINDOW to let fresh air and sunlight into your room.

sentence.japanese: 窓を 開けてください。
sentence.reading: まどを あけてください。

fact: The word 増税 (zōzei), meaning 'tax increase,' often appears in Japanese news. You'll also hear 人口が増える ('the population increases').
memory: Imagine stacking MORE AND MORE blocks until the tower keeps GROWING taller. It continues to INCREASE.

sentence.japanese: 最近 外国人が 増えています。
sentence.reading: さいきん がいこくじんが ふえています。
sentence.english: Recently, the number of foreign visitors has increased.

fact: When riding the Shinkansen, passengers often ask for 窓側 (window side) or 通路側 (aisle side) seats. The kanji 側 simply means 'side.'
memory: Imagine standing beside a friend. You are always at their SIDE.

sentence.japanese: 右側を 歩いてください。
sentence.reading: みぎがわを あるいてください。
sentence.english: Please walk on the right side.

fact: The phrase 一息つく (hitoiki tsuku), meaning 'to take a short break,' literally means 'to take one breath.' It's a common expression after finishing hard work.
memory: Imagine taking a DEEP BREATH after climbing a mountain. That one breath gives you a chance to REST.

sentence.japanese: 少し 一息 つきましょう。
sentence.reading: すこし ひといき つきましょう。

kunyomi: たば (taba), たば-ねる (taba-neru), つか (tsuka)
fact: The kanji 束 appears in 約束 (yakusoku), meaning 'promise.' Originally, a promise was like tying people together with a bundle or knot that could not easily be broken.
memory: Imagine tying many sticks together into one BUNDLE with a rope. They become stronger when BOUND together.

sentence.japanese: 約束を 忘れないでください。
sentence.reading: やくそくを わすれないでください。

kunyomi: はや-い (haya-i), はや-める (haya-meru), はや-まる (haya-maru), すみ-やか (sumi-yaka)
fact: Japan's famous Shinkansen is known as the 'bullet train' because of its incredible speed. The kanji 速 appears in words like 速度 (speed) and 高速 (high speed).
memory: Imagine a bullet train racing past so FAST that it disappears before you can blink.

sentence.japanese: この 電車は とても 速いです。
sentence.reading: この でんしゃは とても はやいです。
sentence.romaji: kono densha wa totemo hayai desu

fact: The phrase 続きを読む ('Continue reading') appears on countless Japanese websites and e-books. This kanji is essential for describing anything that continues.
memory: Imagine a long ROAD that never seems to end. It CONTINUES far beyond the horizon.

sentence.japanese: 日本語の 勉強を 続けます。
sentence.reading: にほんごの べんきょうを つづけます。

fact: The word 存在 (sonzai), meaning 'existence,' is one of the most common compounds using this kanji. You'll also hear ご存じですか? ('Do you know?') in polite Japanese.
memory: Imagine protecting a precious treasure so it continues to EXIST for future generations. Preserving something keeps it alive.

sentence.japanese: この 建物は 今も 存在しています。
sentence.reading: この たてものは いまも そんざいしています。
sentence.romaji: kono tatemono wa ima mo sonzai shiteimasu

fact: The expression その他 (sono ta), meaning 'others,' appears everywhere from official documents to shopping websites. It's one of the most practical kanji for everyday reading.
memory: Imagine choosing ONE PERSON from a group, while everyone else becomes the OTHERS.

sentence.japanese: 他に 質問は ありますか。
sentence.reading: ほかに しつもんは ありますか。
sentence.romaji: hoka ni shitsumon wa arimasu ka
sentence.english: Do you have any other questions?

fact: The kanji 打 appears in many modern words such as 打つ (to hit), 打鍵 (typing on a keyboard), and 野球の打者 (baseball batter). In Japan, baseball is one of the country's most popular sports.
memory: Imagine swinging a baseball bat and HITTING the ball with a loud crack. The hand radical reminds you that someone is STRIKING something.

sentence.japanese: ボールを 強く 打ちました。
sentence.reading: ボールを つよく うちました。

fact: The symbol 'vs.' in Japanese sports schedules is usually written with 対. You'll also see it in words like 対話 (dialogue) and 対策 (countermeasure).
memory: Imagine TWO PEOPLE standing FACE TO FACE. They are directly OPPOSITE each other, ready for a match.

sentence.japanese: 私は その 意見に 反対です。
sentence.reading: わたしは その いけんに はんたいです。
sentence.romaji: watashi wa sono iken ni hantai desu

kunyomi: しりぞ-く (shirizo-ku), しりぞ-ける (shirizo-keru), の-く (no-ku)
fact: The kanji 退 appears in useful everyday words such as 退院 (hospital discharge), 退職 (retirement), and 退出 (leaving a room).
memory: Imagine taking several STEPS BACKWARD away from danger. You RETREAT and LEAVE the area safely.

sentence.japanese: 会議の 前に 部屋を 退出しました。
sentence.reading: かいぎの まえに へやを たいしゅつしました。
sentence.romaji: kaigi no mae ni heya o taishutsu shimashita
sentence.english: I left the room before the meeting.

fact: 宅 is commonly used in words related to homes and deliveries. 宅配便 (takkyūbin) refers to Japan's famous home delivery service, known for its speed and reliability.
memory: Imagine a ROOF protecting a comfortable HOME where your family waits for you every evening.

sentence.japanese: 七時に 帰宅しました。
sentence.reading: しちじに きたくしました。
sentence.english: I returned home at seven o'clock.
