fact: The suffix ～達 (たち) is commonly added to people or animals to make them plural, such as 私達 (we) and 子供達 (children). The kanji also appears in words like 到達 (arrival) and 発達 (development).
memory: Imagine finally REACHING the top of a mountain after a long climb. You have ACCOMPLISHED your goal.

sentence.japanese: 友達と 映画を 見ました。
sentence.reading: ともだちと えいがを みました。
sentence.english: I watched a movie with my friend.

fact: The kanji 単 appears in many everyday words such as 簡単 (easy), 単語 (vocabulary word), and 単位 (unit). It often carries the idea of 'one' or 'not complicated.'
memory: Imagine solving ONE SIMPLE puzzle piece instead of a thousand. A SINGLE piece is much EASIER to handle.

sentence.japanese: この 問題は とても 簡単です。
sentence.reading: この もんだいは とても かんたんです。
sentence.romaji: kono mondai wa totemo kantan desu

fact: 探 is commonly used in everyday Japanese in words like 探す (to search), 探検 (exploration), and 探偵 (detective). Detective stories and anime often feature this kanji.
memory: Imagine using your HAND to SEARCH every corner of a room until you finally find your lost keys.

sentence.japanese: 財布を 探しています。
sentence.reading: さいふを さがしています。
sentence.english: I am looking for my wallet.

fact: The polite phrase お断りします ('I must decline') is commonly used in business Japanese. The kanji also appears in 判断 (judgment) and 断水 (water outage).
memory: Imagine using a sharp sword to CUT a rope. Once it's cut, you've made a DECISION and REFUSED to continue.

sentence.japanese: その 誘いを 丁寧に 断りました。
sentence.reading: その さそいを ていねいに ことわりました。
sentence.romaji: sono sasoi o teinei ni kotowarimashita

fact: Martial arts like Judo, Karate, and Kendo use the 段 ranking system. A black belt often begins at 初段 (first dan).
memory: Imagine climbing a STAIRCASE one STEP at a time. Every step takes you to a higher LEVEL.

sentence.japanese: 階段を 上がってください。
sentence.reading: かいだんを あがってください。

fact: The kanji 談 appears in words like 相談 (consultation), 会談 (conference), and 冗談 (joke). It almost always relates to speaking or conversation.
memory: Imagine two people TALKING over a warm cup of tea. Their friendly DISCUSSION continues for hours.

sentence.japanese: 先生に 相談しました。
sentence.reading: せんせいに そうだんしました。

fact: The kanji 値 appears everywhere in Japan—from supermarket price tags to stock market reports. Words like 値段 (price) and 価値 (value) are among the most common shopping and business vocabulary.
memory: Imagine looking at a PRICE TAG hanging from a product. That little tag tells you its VALUE.

sentence.japanese: この シャツの 値段は 五千円です。
sentence.reading: この シャツの ねだんは ごせんえんです。
sentence.romaji: kono shatsu no nedan wa gosen en desu
sentence.english: The price of this shirt is 5,000 yen.

kunyomi: は-じる (ha-jiru), は-ずかしい (ha-zukashii), はじ (haji)
fact: Japanese culture places great importance on avoiding 恥 (shame). The expression 恥ずかしい ('I'm embarrassed') is heard frequently in daily conversation.
memory: Imagine your FACE turning bright red after making a mistake in front of everyone. That feeling is EMBARRASSMENT.

sentence.japanese: みんなの 前で 間違えて 恥ずかしかったです。
sentence.reading: みんなの まえで まちがえて はずかしかったです。
sentence.romaji: minna no mae de machigaete hazukashikatta desu
sentence.english: I was embarrassed after making a mistake in front of everyone.

fact: You'll often hear 「ここに置いてください」 ('Please put it here') in restaurants, stores, and offices. It's one of the most useful action verbs in Japanese.
memory: Imagine carefully PLACING a BOOK on a TABLE. Once it's there, you LEAVE it in that spot.

sentence.japanese: 荷物を ここに 置いてください。
sentence.reading: にもつを ここに おいてください。
sentence.romaji: nimotsu o koko ni oite kudasai

kunyomi: おく-れる (oku-reru), おそ-い (oso-i), おく-らす (oku-rasu)
fact: Japan's trains are famous for punctuality, so when one is even a few minutes 遅れる (late), it often becomes national news. Railway companies even issue delay certificates for commuters.
memory: Imagine arriving at the station just after your train leaves. You're too LATE and now have to wait.

sentence.japanese: 学校に 遅れないでください。
sentence.reading: がっこうに おくれないでください。
sentence.english: Please don't be late for school.

kunyomi: しら-べる (shira-beru), ととの-う (totono-u), ととの-える (totono-eru)
fact: The kanji 調 is used in many everyday words such as 調べる (to investigate), 強調 (emphasis), and 体調 (physical condition). It often expresses the idea of checking or bringing something into balance.
memory: Imagine a detective carefully INVESTIGATING every clue, then ADJUSTING the pieces until the mystery is solved.

sentence.japanese: 今日は 体調が いいです。
sentence.reading: きょうは たいちょうが いいです。
sentence.romaji: kyou wa taichou ga ii desu

kunyomi: いただ-く (itada-ku), いただき (itadaki)
fact: The humble verb 頂く (いただく) literally means 'to receive,' but it is also said before meals in Japan as 『いただきます』, expressing gratitude to everyone involved in preparing the food.
memory: Imagine standing on the TOP of a mountain and RECEIVING a gift from the sky. The mountain's SUMMIT helps you remember 頂.

sentence.japanese: 先生から 本を 頂きました。
sentence.reading: せんせいから ほんを いただきました。
sentence.english: I received a book from my teacher.

kunyomi: なお-す (nao-su), なお-る (nao-ru), ただ-ちに (tada-chini)
fact: The kanji 直 appears in useful words like 直線 (straight line), 正直 (honest), and 直す (to fix). It often expresses the idea of making something correct or straight.
memory: Imagine bending a crooked stick until it becomes perfectly STRAIGHT. You FIX it by making it DIRECT again.

sentence.japanese: 壊れた 時計を 直しました。
sentence.reading: こわれた とけいを なおしました。

fact: The kanji 追 is found in words like 追跡 (pursuit), 追加 (addition), and 追放 (banishment). It always carries the idea of following after something.
memory: Imagine running AFTER someone who dropped their wallet. You CHASE them to return it.

sentence.japanese: 犬が ボールを 追っています。
sentence.reading: いぬが ボールを おっています。
sentence.english: The dog is chasing the ball.

kunyomi: いた-い (ita-i), いた-む (ita-mu), いた-める (ita-meru)
fact: The expression 『痛い！』 ('Ouch!') is one of the first Japanese words children learn. The kanji also appears in words like 頭痛 (headache) and 腹痛 (stomachache).
memory: Imagine accidentally hitting your thumb with a hammer. The sharp PAIN makes you instantly shout 'Ouch!'

sentence.japanese: 今日は 頭が 痛いです。
sentence.reading: きょうは あたまが いたいです。
sentence.romaji: kyou wa atama ga itai desu

kunyomi: さだ-める (sada-meru), さだ-まる (sada-maru), さだ-か (sada-ka)
fact: The kanji 定 appears in many everyday words such as 予定 (schedule), 定食 (set meal), and 安定 (stability). It usually expresses something fixed or decided.
memory: Imagine putting a FLAG into the ground to mark the FINAL DECISION. Once it's planted, everything is SET.

sentence.japanese: 来週の 予定を 決めました。
sentence.reading: らいしゅうの よていを きめました。

fact: Traditional Japanese gardens (日本庭園) are carefully designed to represent mountains, rivers, and forests in miniature. Even a small garden can symbolize an entire natural landscape.
memory: Imagine stepping outside your HOUSE into a peaceful GARDEN filled with bonsai trees and a tiny pond. That's your 庭.

sentence.japanese: 庭に きれいな 花が 咲いています。
sentence.reading: にわに きれいな はなが さいています。
sentence.romaji: niwa ni kirei na hana ga saiteimasu
sentence.english: Beautiful flowers are blooming in the garden.

fact: The word 程 is often used after numbers to mean 'about' or 'approximately,' such as 一時間ほど ('about one hour'). It's an essential everyday expression.
memory: Imagine filling a measuring cup to a certain LEVEL. That amount shows the DEGREE or EXTENT of something.

sentence.japanese: 駅まで 十分ほど 歩きます。
sentence.reading: えきまで じゅっぷんほど あるきます。
sentence.english: It's about a ten-minute walk to the station.

fact: You'll often see 適 in words like 最適 (optimal), 適切 (appropriate), and 快適 (comfortable). It describes something that fits perfectly for a situation.
memory: Imagine trying on several pairs of shoes until one FITS PERFECTLY. That's the MOST SUITABLE choice.

sentence.japanese: この 靴は 山登りに 適しています。
sentence.reading: この くつは やまのぼりに てきしています。
sentence.romaji: kono kutsu wa yamanobori ni tekishiteimasu
sentence.english: These shoes are suitable for mountain climbing.

fact: Japanese students receive 点 (points) on exams, and convenience stores often award ポイント (points) for purchases. The kanji is everywhere in daily life.
memory: Imagine drawing a single DOT on a blank page. One tiny POINT can mark the beginning of everything.

sentence.japanese: テストで 百点を 取りました。
sentence.reading: テストで ひゃくてんを とりました。
sentence.english: I got a perfect score on the test.

kunyomi: つた-える (tsuta-eru), つた-わる (tsuta-waru), つた-う (tsuta-u)
fact: The kanji 伝 appears in words like 電話 (telephone), 伝説 (legend), and 宣伝 (advertisement). It always carries the idea of passing something from one person or place to another.
memory: Imagine passing a MESSAGE from one PERSON to the next until everyone knows it. That's how information is CONVEYED.

sentence.japanese: 先生に この メッセージを 伝えてください。
sentence.reading: せんせいに この メッセージを つたえてください。
sentence.romaji: sensei ni kono messeeji o tsutaete kudasai
sentence.english: Please convey this message to the teacher.

fact: Although 徒 originally meant 'follower' or 'companion,' you'll most often see it in words like 生徒 (student), 徒歩 (on foot), and 信徒 (believer).
memory: Imagine a GROUP OF STUDENTS following their teacher on foot. They are all FOLLOWERS walking together.

sentence.japanese: 学校まで 徒歩で 行きます。
sentence.reading: がっこうまで とほで いきます。
sentence.english: I go to school on foot.

fact: Japan is connected by thousands of bridges, so the verb 渡る ('to cross') is heard constantly. The kanji also means 'to hand something over' to another person.
memory: Imagine CROSSING a long bridge over a river, then HANDING a package to a friend waiting on the other side.

sentence.japanese: 橋を 渡って 駅へ 行きます。
sentence.reading: はしを わたって えきへ いきます。
sentence.romaji: hashi o watatte eki e ikimasu
sentence.english: I cross the bridge to go to the station.

fact: Every summer, thousands of people 登る (climb) Mount Fuji. The kanji also appears in 登録 (registration) and 登場 (appearance on stage).
memory: Imagine CLIMBING a mountain step by step until you finally reach the summit.

sentence.japanese: 来年 富士山に 登りたいです。
sentence.reading: らいねん ふじさんに のぼりたいです。
sentence.english: I want to climb Mount Fuji next year.

fact: The word 途中 (tochū), meaning 'on the way,' is extremely common in Japanese. You'll hear it while traveling, commuting, or giving directions.
memory: Imagine walking along a ROAD and stopping HALFWAY through your journey. You are still ON THE WAY.

sentence.japanese: 途中で 雨が 降り始めました。
sentence.reading: とちゅうで あめが ふりはじめました。
sentence.english: It started raining on the way.

fact: The expression 努力 (doryoku), meaning 'effort,' is one of the most motivating words in Japanese. You'll often hear 『努力は裏切らない』 ('Hard work never betrays you').
memory: Imagine pushing a heavy boulder uphill with all your STRENGTH. That constant hard work represents EFFORT.

sentence.japanese: 毎日 努力しています。
sentence.reading: まいにち どりょくしています。
sentence.english: I make an effort every day.

fact: Japanese often avoid showing strong anger openly. Instead of saying someone is 怒っている ('angry'), people may describe them as 機嫌が悪い ('in a bad mood') to sound softer.
memory: Imagine your HEART burning with FIRE when someone cuts in line. That burning feeling is ANGER.

sentence.japanese: 先生は とても 怒っています。
sentence.reading: せんせいは とても おこっています。

fact: The kanji 倒 appears in words like 転倒 (falling down) and 倒産 (bankruptcy). It can describe both physical collapse and financial failure.
memory: Imagine a row of dominoes FALLING one after another. Everything COLLAPSES in seconds.

sentence.japanese: 木が 強い風で 倒れました。
sentence.reading: きが つよいかぜで たおれました。
sentence.romaji: ki ga tsuyoi kaze de taoremashita
sentence.english: The tree fell because of the strong wind.

fact: Baseball is one of Japan's most popular sports, and 投手 (pitcher) literally means 'the person who throws.' The kanji is also used in words like 投票 (voting).
memory: Imagine THROWING a baseball with all your strength. Your HAND sends it flying through the air.

sentence.japanese: ボールを 遠くへ 投げました。
sentence.reading: ボールを とおくへ なげました。
sentence.english: I threw the ball far away.

fact: Japan has one of the world's lowest crime rates, but you'll still see signs warning against 盗難 (theft) at bicycle parking areas and train stations.
memory: Imagine a THIEF quietly taking a bicycle in the middle of the night. The act of STEALING is 盗.

sentence.japanese: 自転車が 盗まれました。
sentence.reading: じてんしゃが ぬすまれました。

fact: The kanji 当 appears in many everyday words such as 本当 (truth), 当日 (the day itself), and 担当 (person in charge). It often conveys the idea of being correct, appropriate, or matching.
memory: Imagine throwing a dart that lands exactly in the CENTER of the target. You HIT the RIGHT spot.

sentence.japanese: その 答えは 本当です。
sentence.reading: その こたえは ほんとうです。

fact: The kanji 等 is extremely common in signs, notices, and official documents. After a noun, it often means 'and others' or 'etc.', as in 学生等 ('students, etc.').
memory: Imagine several people standing on the SAME LEVEL podium. They are all EQUAL, belonging to the same CLASS.

sentence.japanese: 本や 雑誌等を 買いました。
sentence.reading: ほんや ざっしなどを かいました。
sentence.romaji: hon ya zasshi nado o kaimashita
sentence.english: I bought books, magazines, and other things.

fact: The kanji 到 appears in words like 到着 (arrival) and 到達 (reaching a goal). It always conveys successfully getting to a destination or objective.
memory: Imagine finally ARRIVING at the top of a mountain after a long journey. You've REACHED your destination.

sentence.japanese: 電車が 駅に 到着しました。
sentence.reading: でんしゃが えきに とうちゃくしました。
sentence.romaji: densha ga eki ni touchaku shimashita
sentence.english: The train arrived at the station.

kunyomi: に-げる (ni-geru), に-がす (ni-gasu), のが-す (noga-su), のが-れる (noga-reru)
fact: The phrase 現実逃避 (genjitsu tōhi), meaning 'escaping from reality,' is a humorous expression commonly used online in Japan.
memory: Imagine a rabbit RUNNING AWAY from a fox as fast as it can. It ESCAPES just in time.

sentence.japanese: 猫が 外へ 逃げました。
sentence.reading: ねこが そとへ にげました。

fact: The kanji 得 is found in words like 得意 (good at), 納得 (understanding), and お得 (good bargain). It's one of the most useful kanji for shopping and everyday conversation.
memory: Imagine finding a GOLD COIN on the ground. You GAIN something valuable without expecting it.

sentence.japanese: 彼は 数学が 得意です。
sentence.reading: かれは すうがくが とくいです。
sentence.romaji: kare wa suugaku ga tokui desu

fact: The kanji 突 appears in words like 突然 (suddenly), 突撃 (charge), and 衝突 (collision). It often describes something happening abruptly or forcefully.
memory: Imagine a SPEAR PIERCING through a wall with a sudden THRUST. The action is fast and unexpected.

sentence.japanese: 突然 雨が 降り始めました。
sentence.reading: とつぜん あめが ふりはじめました。

fact: The kanji 内 appears in countless everyday words such as 家内 (wife), 国内 (domestic), and 案内 (guidance). It always carries the idea of being 'inside' or 'within.'
memory: Imagine walking INSIDE a house and closing the door behind you. Everything is now WITHIN the building.

sentence.japanese: 部屋の 中に 入ってください。
sentence.reading: へやの なかに はいってください。
sentence.romaji: heya no naka ni haitte kudasai

kunyomi: むずか-しい (muzuka-shii), かた-い (kata-i)
fact: The word 難しい (muzukashii), meaning 'difficult,' is one of the first adjectives Japanese learners memorize. The kanji also appears in words like 避難 (evacuation) and 災難 (disaster).
memory: Imagine trying to climb a steep mountain with many obstacles. The journey is DIFFICULT and full of HARDSHIP.

sentence.japanese: この 問題は とても 難しいです。
sentence.reading: この もんだいは とても むずかしいです。
sentence.romaji: kono mondai wa totemo muzukashii desu

fact: The kanji 任 is used in common business words such as 責任 (responsibility), 主任 (supervisor), and 任せる (to entrust). It often relates to duties and trust.
memory: Imagine someone HANDING YOU the keys to an office because they TRUST you with the RESPONSIBILITY.

sentence.japanese: この 仕事を 私に 任せてください。
sentence.reading: この しごとを わたしに まかせてください。
sentence.romaji: kono shigoto o watashi ni makasete kudasai
sentence.english: Please leave this job to me.

kunyomi: みと-める (mito-meru), したた-める (shitatameru)
fact: The kanji 認 appears in words such as 認識 (recognition), 承認 (approval), and 確認 (confirmation). It's one of the most important business kanji in Japanese.
memory: Imagine a MANAGER carefully CHECKING a document before stamping it APPROVED. He RECOGNIZES that everything is correct.

sentence.japanese: 内容を 確認してください。
sentence.reading: ないようを かくにんしてください。

fact: Cats are beloved in Japan. Places like 'Cat Island' (Aoshima) and the famous 招き猫 (maneki-neko, lucky cat) have made 猫 one of Japan's most iconic animals.
memory: Imagine a CAT quietly stretching in the sunshine before curling up for a nap. The animal itself is easy to picture whenever you see 猫.

sentence.japanese: 猫が 窓の 外で 寝ています。
sentence.reading: ねこが まどの そとで ねています。
sentence.romaji: neko ga mado no soto de neteimasu
sentence.english: A cat is sleeping outside the window.

fact: The kanji 熱 appears in everyday words like 熱心 (enthusiastic), 情熱 (passion), and 発熱 (fever). It can describe both physical heat and emotional intensity.
memory: Imagine touching a HOT kettle. The intense HEAT instantly makes you pull your hand away. That burning feeling is 熱.

sentence.japanese: 今日は 熱が あります。
sentence.reading: きょうは ねつが あります。

fact: The kanji 念 appears in words like 記念 (commemoration), 念願 (long-cherished wish), and 残念 (regret). It always relates to thoughts or feelings held in the mind.
memory: Imagine carefully HOLDING a single THOUGHT in your HEART without letting it disappear. That's 念.

sentence.japanese: 旅行の 記念に 写真を 撮りました。
sentence.reading: りょこうの きねんに しゃしんを とりました。
sentence.romaji: ryokou no kinen ni shashin o torimashita
sentence.english: I took a photo to remember the trip.

fact: The kanji 能 is found in words like 可能 (possible), 能力 (ability), and 機能 (function). It is also the name of Japan's traditional Noh theater (能).
memory: Imagine unlocking a SUPERPOWER inside yourself. That hidden power represents your ABILITY.

sentence.japanese: 彼は 英語を 話す 能力が あります。
sentence.reading: かれは えいごを はなす のうりょくが あります。
sentence.romaji: kare wa eigo o hanasu nouryoku ga arimasu
sentence.english: He has the ability to speak English.

fact: The kanji 破 appears in words like 破壊 (destruction), 突破 (breakthrough), and 破れる (to tear). It often describes breaking through obstacles.
memory: Imagine SMASHING a brick wall with a hammer. The wall BREAKS apart into pieces.

sentence.japanese: 紙を 破らないでください。
sentence.reading: かみを やぶらないでください。

onyomi: ハイ (hai), バイ (bai), パイ (pai)
fact: 杯 is the counter used for drinks in Japanese. It also appears in famous competitions like 世界杯 (World Cup) and 乾杯 (Cheers!), making it a very practical kanji.
memory: Imagine raising a CUP full of tea and saying 'CHEERS!' before taking a sip. The CUP reminds you of 杯.

sentence.japanese: コーヒーを 一杯 お願いします。
sentence.reading: コーヒーを いっぱい おねがいします。

kunyomi: せ (se), せい (sei), そむ-く (somu-ku), そむ-ける (somu-keru)
fact: The kanji 背 is commonly used in the expression 背が高い (se ga takai), meaning 'to be tall.' It literally refers to a person's back or height rather than just stature.
memory: Imagine measuring someone's HEIGHT by placing a ruler against their BACK. Their BACK helps determine how TALL they are.

sentence.japanese: 兄は 背が 高いです。
sentence.reading: あには せが たかいです。
sentence.romaji: ani wa se ga takai desu

fact: The kanji 配 appears in many practical words such as 配達 (delivery), 心配 (worry), and 宅配 (home delivery). It often carries the idea of distributing or paying attention to something.
memory: Imagine a delivery driver HANDING OUT packages to every house on the street. He DISTRIBUTES them one by one.

sentence.japanese: 先生が プリントを 配りました。
sentence.reading: せんせいが プリントを くばりました。
sentence.english: The teacher handed out the worksheets.

fact: Japan is famous for beautifully designed gift boxes. Traditional sweets, bento meals, and souvenirs are often packaged in elegant 箱 that are almost works of art.
memory: Imagine opening a beautifully wrapped GIFT BOX to discover a surprise inside. The BOX itself is 箱.

sentence.japanese: 箱の 中に お菓子が 入っています。
sentence.reading: はこの なかに おかしが はいっています。
sentence.romaji: hako no naka ni okashi ga haitteimasu
sentence.english: There are sweets inside the box.

fact: Japanese hairstyles often appear in fashion magazines using words like 長髪 (long hair) and 黒髪 (black hair). Naturally black hair has traditionally been admired in Japanese culture.
memory: Imagine COMBING your HAIR in front of a mirror every morning. Those strands remind you of 髪.

sentence.japanese: 昨日 髪を 切りました。
sentence.reading: きのう かみを きりました。

kunyomi: ぬ-く (nu-ku), ぬ-ける (nu-keru), ぬ-かす (nu-kasu), ぬ-かる (nu-karu)
fact: The kanji 抜 is used in many common expressions such as 抜く (to pull out), 手を抜く (to cut corners), and 抜群 (outstanding). It often implies removing or standing out from others.
memory: Imagine PULLING a carrot OUT of the ground with both hands. It comes completely OUT of the soil.

sentence.japanese: 庭の 草を 抜きました。
sentence.reading: にわの くさを ぬきました。
sentence.english: I pulled the weeds out of the garden.

fact: The kanji 判 appears in common words like 判断 (judgment), 判決 (court decision), and 批判 (criticism). In Japan, personal seals (判子・はんこ) are also written with this kanji.
memory: Imagine a JUDGE slamming a stamp onto an official document. That stamp represents making a DECISION.

sentence.japanese: 急いで 判断しないでください。
sentence.reading: いそいで はんだんしないでください。
sentence.english: Please don't make a judgment in a hurry.

fact: The kanji 反 appears in many everyday words including 反対 (opposition), 反応 (reaction), and 違反 (violation). It usually expresses the idea of 'against' or 'opposite.'
memory: Imagine two arrows pointing in OPPOSITE directions. They completely OPPOSE each other.

sentence.japanese: 私は その 意見に 反対です。
sentence.reading: わたしは その いけんに はんたいです。
sentence.romaji: watashi wa sono iken ni hantai desu

fact: The kanji 犯 is used in words like 犯人 (criminal), 犯罪 (crime), and 違反 (violation). It almost always refers to breaking laws or rules.
memory: Imagine a THIEF being caught by the police after committing a CRIME. That illegal act is 犯.

sentence.japanese: その 犯人は 捕まりました。
sentence.reading: その はんにんは つかまりました。

fact: You'll encounter 晩 every day in greetings like 今晩は ('Good evening') and words like 晩ご飯 (dinner). It specifically refers to the later part of the day.
memory: Imagine the SUN slowly disappearing as the EVENING sky turns orange. Night is about to begin.

sentence.japanese: 毎晩 本を 読みます。
sentence.reading: まいばん ほんを よみます。
sentence.english: I read a book every night.

fact: The kanji 番 is everywhere in Japan—from train platform numbers (ホーム1番) to TV programs (番組) and waiting lines where people take a numbered ticket.
memory: Imagine taking a NUMBERED ticket at a busy restaurant and waiting patiently for your TURN.

sentence.japanese: 私の 番が 来ました。
sentence.reading: わたしの ばんが きました。

fact: The kanji 否 is commonly seen in formal Japanese, especially in words like 否定 (denial) and 可否 (approval or disapproval). It expresses the idea of 'no' or rejection.
memory: Imagine someone firmly SHAKING their head and saying 'NO!' They completely DENY the request.

sentence.japanese: 彼は その 話を 否定しました。
sentence.reading: かれは その はを ひていしました。
sentence.romaji: kare wa sono hanashi o hitei shimashita

fact: The word 彼 (kare) means 'he,' but in modern Japanese it also commonly means 'boyfriend.' Likewise, 彼女 (kanojo) means both 'she' and 'girlfriend.'
memory: Imagine pointing to a BOY standing over THERE and saying, 'HE is my friend.' That distant person is 彼.

sentence.japanese: 彼は 日本語が 上手です。
sentence.reading: かれは にほんごが じょうずです。
sentence.romaji: kare wa nihongo ga jouzu desu

kunyomi: かな-しい (kana-shii), かな-しむ (kana-shimu)
fact: The kanji 悲 appears in words like 悲劇 (tragedy) and 悲鳴 (scream). It represents deep sadness or grief rather than simple disappointment.
memory: Imagine TEARS falling from someone's eyes after hearing sad news. Their HEART is filled with SORROW.

sentence.japanese: その 映画は とても 悲しかったです。
sentence.reading: その えいがは とても かかったです。
sentence.romaji: sono eiga wa totemo kanashikatta desu

kunyomi: つか-れる (tsuka-reru), つか-らす (tsuka-rasu)
fact: One of the most useful expressions in Japanese workplaces is お疲れ様です ('Thank you for your hard work'). Although it contains 疲 ('tired'), it is actually a polite greeting and expression of appreciation.
memory: Imagine COLLAPSING onto a sofa after a long day because your BODY is completely TIRED.

sentence.japanese: 今日は とても 疲れました。
sentence.reading: きょうは とても つかれました。

fact: The kanji 費 appears in many financial words such as 学費 (tuition), 消費 (consumption), and 交通費 (transportation expenses). It almost always relates to spending money.
memory: Imagine opening your WALLET and watching MONEY disappear to pay your bills. That's an EXPENSE.

sentence.japanese: 交通費は 会社が 払います。
sentence.reading: こうつうひは かいしゃが はらいます。
sentence.english: The company pays the transportation expenses.

fact: The kanji 非 is widely used in formal Japanese. You'll encounter it in words like 非常 (emergency), 非公開 (private), and 非難 (criticism). It generally carries the meaning of 'not' or 'wrong.'
memory: Imagine a giant red 'X' over a sign. It means NOT ALLOWED or WRONG. That image represents 非.

words.meaning: Private, Not open to the public

sentence.japanese: 非常口は あちらです。
sentence.reading: ひじょうぐちは あちらです。
sentence.english: The emergency exit is over there.

fact: The kanji 飛 appears in words like 飛行機 (airplane), 飛行場 (airport), and 飛ぶ (to fly). It is essential vocabulary for travel in Japan.
memory: Imagine a BIRD spreading its wings and FLYING high into the sky. That's the image behind 飛.

sentence.japanese: 鳥が 空を 飛んでいます。
sentence.reading: とりが そらを とんでいます。
sentence.english: A bird is flying in the sky.

kunyomi: そな-える (sona-eru), そな-わる (sona-waru)
fact: Japan's earthquake preparedness is famous worldwide. The kanji 備 appears in words like 準備 (preparation), 設備 (facilities), and 防備 (defense).
memory: Imagine packing an EMERGENCY BAG before a trip. You PREPARE everything you might need.

sentence.japanese: 旅行の 準備を しています。
sentence.reading: りょこうの じゅんびを しています。
sentence.english: I am preparing for the trip.

fact: The kanji 美 appears in words like 美術 (art), 美人 (beautiful person), and 美容 (beauty care). It symbolizes beauty in both appearance and art.
memory: Imagine admiring a BEAUTIFUL cherry blossom tree in full bloom. The scene is filled with BEAUTY.

sentence.japanese: 京都には 美しい お寺が 多いです。
sentence.reading: きょうとには うつくしい おてらが おおいです。
sentence.romaji: Kyouto ni wa utsukushii otera ga ooi desu

fact: The kanji 必 appears in extremely common words such as 必要 (necessary), 必死 (desperate), and 必ず (certainly). It conveys certainty or something that cannot be avoided.
memory: Imagine a TEACHER saying, 'You MUST bring your homework tomorrow.' It is ABSOLUTELY NECESSARY.

sentence.japanese: 明日は 必ず 来てください。
sentence.reading: あしたは かならず きてください。
sentence.english: Please be sure to come tomorrow.

kunyomi: おもて (omote), あらわ-す (arawa-su), あらわ-れる (arawa-reru)
fact: The kanji 表 is one of the most versatile in Japanese. It appears in words like 発表 (presentation), 表情 (facial expression), and 表 (table/chart), all connected by the idea of 'bringing something to the surface.'
memory: Imagine opening a BOOK to a TABLE full of information. Everything is displayed openly on the SURFACE for everyone to see.

sentence.japanese: 来週 研究を 発表します。
sentence.reading: らいしゅう けんきゅうを はっぴょうします。
sentence.english: I will give a presentation next week.

fact: The kanji 貧 appears in words like 貧乏 (poor), 貧困 (poverty), and 貧血 (anemia). Originally, it described a lack of wealth or resources.
memory: Imagine opening an EMPTY WALLET with only one coin inside. Having very little money means POVERTY.

sentence.japanese: 彼は 貧しい 家庭で 育ちました。
sentence.reading: かれは まずしい かていで そだちました。
sentence.romaji: kare wa mazushii katei de sodachimashita
sentence.english: He grew up in a poor family.

fact: The kanji 付 is extremely common in Japanese. It appears in words like 受付 (reception), 日付 (date), and 添付 (attachment), all involving something being attached or assigned.
memory: Imagine ATTACHING a sticky note onto a notebook. Once it's stuck ON, it stays WITH the notebook.

sentence.japanese: 名前を 紙に 書いて 付けてください。
sentence.reading: なまえを かみに かいて つけてください。
sentence.romaji: namae o kami ni kaite tsukete kudasai
sentence.english: Please write your name and attach it to the paper.

fact: The kanji 婦 appears in words like 夫婦 (married couple), 主婦 (homemaker), and 婦人 (lady). It generally refers to adult women, especially in family or social contexts.
memory: Imagine a MARRIED WOMAN welcoming her family home with a warm smile. She represents the idea of WIFE or LADY.

sentence.japanese: その 夫婦は とても 仲が 良いです。
sentence.reading: その ふうふは とても なかが よいです。
sentence.romaji: sono fuufu wa totemo naka ga yoi desu
sentence.english: That married couple gets along very well.

kunyomi: う-く (u-ku), う-かぶ (u-kabu), う-かれる (u-kareru), う-かべる (u-kaberu)
fact: The kanji 浮 is used in words like 浮かぶ (to float), 浮世絵 (ukiyo-e), and 浮気 (unfaithfulness). Its core meaning is something that rises or floats above the surface.
memory: Imagine a WOODEN BOAT FLOATING peacefully on a calm lake. It gently RISES and falls with the waves.

sentence.japanese: 葉っぱが 水に 浮いています。
sentence.reading: はっぱが みずに ういています。
sentence.english: A leaf is floating on the water.

kunyomi: ま-ける (ma-keru), ま-かす (ma-kasu), お-う (o-u)
fact: The kanji 負 appears in many everyday words such as 勝負 (match), 負担 (burden), and 責任を負う (to bear responsibility). It can mean both 'to lose' and 'to carry a burden.'
memory: Imagine carrying a HEAVY BACKPACK after LOSING a competition. The heavy load reminds you of a BURDEN.

sentence.japanese: 昨日の 試合で 負けました。
sentence.reading: きのうの しあいで まけました。

fact: Traditional Japanese dances, such as those performed during festivals and in Kabuki, often use the kanji 舞. It also appears in words like 舞台 (stage).
memory: Imagine a DANCER gracefully spinning across a STAGE while colorful cherry blossom petals dance through the air.

sentence.japanese: 桜の 花びらが 空に 舞っています。
sentence.reading: さくらの はなびらが そらに まっています。
sentence.romaji: sakura no hanabira ga sora ni matteimasu
sentence.english: Cherry blossom petals are dancing through the sky.

fact: The kanji 部 is extremely common in schools and companies. It appears in words like 部屋 (room), 部長 (department manager), and 部活動 (school club).
memory: Imagine a COMPANY divided into different DEPARTMENTS, each responsible for one SECTION of the business.

sentence.japanese: 私は テニス部に 入っています。
sentence.reading: わたしは テニスぶに はいっています。
sentence.romaji: watashi wa tenisu bu ni haitteimasu
sentence.english: I belong to the tennis club.

fact: The kanji 福 symbolizes happiness and good fortune. During the Setsubun festival, Japanese people throw beans while shouting 『福は内！鬼は外！』 ('Fortune in! Demons out!').
memory: Imagine a TREASURE CHEST overflowing with GOLD and HAPPINESS. It brings GOOD FORTUNE to everyone around it.

sentence.japanese: 新しい 年に 幸福を 願います。
sentence.reading: あたらしい としに こうふくを ねがいます。
sentence.romaji: atarashii toshi ni koufuku o negaimasu
sentence.english: I wish for happiness in the new year.

fact: The word 腹 is used both literally and figuratively in Japanese. Besides meaning 'stomach,' it appears in expressions like 腹が立つ ('to get angry') and 腹を決める ('to make up one's mind').
memory: Imagine rubbing your BELLY because you're HUNGRY. Your STOMACH growls loudly until it's fed.

sentence.japanese: お腹が 空きました。
sentence.reading: おなかが すきました。

fact: The kanji 払 is found in everyday words like 支払う (to pay), 払い戻し (refund), and 払う (to brush or sweep away). Whether paying a bill or brushing off dust, the core idea is 'clearing something away.'
memory: Imagine HANDING money to a cashier until your debt disappears. You've PAID it off and CLEARED the bill.

sentence.japanese: カードで 支払いました。
sentence.reading: カードで しはらいました。

fact: The kanji 平 appears in words like 平和 (peace), 平日 (weekday), and 平均 (average). It often conveys the ideas of balance, calmness, and something level.
memory: Imagine a PERFECTLY FLAT road stretching peacefully to the horizon. A FLAT road symbolizes PEACE and stability.

sentence.japanese: 世界の 平和を 願っています。
sentence.reading: せかいの へいわを ねがっています。

kunyomi: し-める (shi-meru), し-まる (shi-maru), と-じる (to-jiru), と-ざす (to-zasu)
fact: The kanji 閉 appears in words like 閉店 (store closing), 閉会 (closing ceremony), and 閉じる (to close). It always involves something being shut or brought to an end.
memory: Imagine CLOSING a large wooden gate until it is completely SHUT. Nothing can pass through anymore.

sentence.japanese: 窓を 閉めてください。
sentence.reading: まどを しめてください。

fact: While 米 literally means 'rice,' it is also the abbreviation for the United States in Japanese, as seen in 米国 (America). This comes from the phonetic borrowing of 'America' in older Japanese.
memory: Imagine a bowl full of WHITE RICE beside an American flag. One symbol reminds you of both RICE and AMERICA.

sentence.japanese: 日本では 米を よく 食べます。
sentence.reading: にほんでは こめを よく たべます。
sentence.romaji: nihon de wa kome o yoku tabemasu
sentence.english: People eat rice frequently in Japan.

fact: The kanji 変 appears in many everyday words like 変更 (change), 大変 (very difficult), and 変化 (change). Interestingly, 大変 originally meant 'a terrible situation' before becoming a common intensifier meaning 'very.'
memory: Imagine a CHAMELEON suddenly CHANGING colors until it looks STRANGE. The unusual transformation represents 変.

sentence.japanese: 予定が 変わりました。
sentence.reading: よていが かわりました。

fact: The kanji 返 appears in everyday words like 返事 (reply), 返品 (returned item), and 返す (to return). Whether it's a borrowed book or an email, the core idea is 'giving something back.'
memory: Imagine BORROWING a book from a friend and later RETURNING it with a smile. That's the meaning of 返.

sentence.japanese: 図書館へ 本を 返しました。
sentence.reading: としょかんへ ほんを かえしました。
sentence.english: I returned the book to the library.

kunyomi: つか-まえる (tsuka-maeru), つか-まる (tsuka-maru), と-らえる (to-raeru), と-る (to-ru)
fact: The kanji 捕 appears in words like 逮捕 (arrest), 捕まえる (to catch), and 捕獲 (capture). It is commonly used in both police and wildlife contexts.
memory: Imagine a POLICE OFFICER CATCHING a thief after a long chase. The thief has been ARRESTED.

sentence.japanese: 警察は 犯人を 捕まえました。
sentence.reading: けいさつは はんにんを つかまえました。

fact: The kanji 暮 is used in words like 生活 (daily life), 年暮れ (year-end), and 暮らす (to live). It often describes the way people spend their everyday lives.
memory: Imagine a FAMILY LIVING peacefully together from MORNING until SUNSET every day. That's 暮らす.

sentence.japanese: 東京で 一人暮らしを しています。
sentence.reading: とうきょうで ひとりぐらしを しています。

fact: The kanji 報 appears in words like 報告 (report), 情報 (information), and 報道 (news reporting). It is one of the most useful kanji in business and media.
memory: Imagine a REPORTER delivering the latest NEWS to millions of viewers. He is REPORTING important INFORMATION.

sentence.japanese: あとで 結果を 報告します。
sentence.reading: あとで けっかを ほうこくします。
sentence.romaji: ato de kekka o houkoku shimasu
sentence.english: I will report the results later.

kunyomi: はな-す (hana-su), はな-つ (hana-tsu), はな-れる (hana-reru), ほう-る (hou-ru)
fact: The kanji 放 appears in words like 放送 (broadcast), 解放 (liberation), and 放置 (leave alone). It always involves the idea of releasing, letting go, or setting free.
memory: Imagine opening a BIRD CAGE and LETTING the bird FLY FREE into the sky. That act of releasing is 放.

sentence.japanese: 鳥を 空へ 放しました。
sentence.reading: とりを そらへ はました。
sentence.english: I released the bird into the sky.

fact: The kanji 法 appears in countless words such as 法律 (law), 方法 (method), and 文法 (grammar). It can refer to legal rules or simply the way of doing something.
memory: Imagine reading a RULE BOOK that teaches both the LAWS of a country and the METHOD for playing a game. Both ideas are captured by 法.

sentence.japanese: もっと 良い 勉強法を 教えてください。
sentence.reading: もっと いい べんきょうほうを おしえてください。
sentence.romaji: motto ii benkyouhou o oshiete kudasai
sentence.english: Please teach me a better study method.

kunyomi: おとず-れる (otozu-reru), たず-ねる (tazu-neru)
fact: The kanji 訪 appears in words like 訪問 (visit) and 訪日 (visiting Japan). It often describes visiting a person or a place.
memory: Imagine KNOCKING on a FRIEND'S DOOR before paying them a VISIT. That action represents 訪.

sentence.japanese: 来月 京都を 訪れる 予定です。
sentence.reading: らいげつ きょうとを おとずれる よていです。
sentence.romaji: raigetsu Kyouto o otozureru yotei desu
sentence.english: I plan to visit Kyoto next month.

fact: The kanji 亡 commonly appears in respectful expressions like 亡くなる ('to pass away') and 亡者 (the deceased). It is generally used in more formal contexts than 死.
memory: Imagine a PERSON quietly DISAPPEARING beyond the horizon. They have PASSED AWAY and are no longer here.

sentence.japanese: 祖父は 去年 亡くなりました。
sentence.reading: そふは きょねん なくなりました。
sentence.english: My grandfather passed away last year.

fact: The kanji 忘 appears in common expressions like 忘れ物 (lost item) and 忘年会 (year-end party). The latter literally means 'a gathering to forget the year's hardships.'
memory: Imagine LEAVING your BAG on the train because you FORGOT it. The missing bag reminds you of 忘.

sentence.japanese: 宿題を 忘れました。
sentence.reading: しゅくだいを わすれました。

kunyomi: いそが-しい (isoga-shii), せわ-しい (sewa-shii)
fact: The kanji 忙 is one of the most frequently used adjectives in Japanese. Interestingly, it combines the 'heart' radical with 'death,' suggesting that being too busy leaves no time for peace of mind.
memory: Imagine RUNNING from one meeting to another all day with NO TIME TO REST. That's what being BUSY feels like.

sentence.japanese: 今日は とても 忙しいです。
sentence.reading: きょうは とても いそがしいです。

fact: The kanji 望 appears in words like 希望 (hope), 願望 (wish), and 展望台 (observation deck). It represents looking ahead toward something you strongly desire.
memory: Imagine standing on top of a HILL, LOOKING FAR into the distance while HOPING your dreams come true.

sentence.japanese: 将来 日本で 働くことを 望んでいます。
sentence.reading: しょうらい にほんで はたらくことを のぞんでいます。
sentence.romaji: shourai Nihon de hataraku koto o nozondeimasu
sentence.english: I hope to work in Japan in the future.

fact: The kanji 末 appears in words like 週末 (weekend), 年末 (year-end), and 末っ子 (youngest child). It generally refers to the end or final part of something.
memory: Imagine walking to the VERY END of a long road until you reach its TIP. That's the meaning of 末.

sentence.japanese: 週末は 家で ゆっくり 休みます。
sentence.reading: しゅうまつは いえで ゆっくり やすみます。
sentence.romaji: shuumatsu wa ie de yukkuri yasumimasu
sentence.english: I'll relax at home this weekend.

kunyomi: み-ちる (mi-chiru), み-たす (mi-tasu), み-つ (mi-tsu)
fact: The kanji 満 appears in words like 満足 (satisfaction), 満員 (full capacity), and 満点 (perfect score). It conveys the idea of being completely filled.
memory: Imagine POURING WATER into a glass until it is COMPLETELY FULL. Nothing else can fit inside.

sentence.japanese: 電車は 満員でした。
sentence.reading: でんしゃは まんいんでした。

fact: The kanji 未 appears in words like 未来 (future), 未完成 (unfinished), and 未知 (unknown). It always suggests that something has not happened yet.
memory: Imagine an EMPTY CALENDAR waiting to be filled. The FUTURE has NOT YET arrived.

sentence.japanese: 予定は まだ 未定です。
sentence.reading: よていは まだ みていです。
sentence.english: The schedule hasn't been decided yet.

fact: The kanji 眠 appears in words like 睡眠 (sleep), 不眠 (insomnia), and 冬眠 (hibernation). It refers to sleep in both humans and animals.
memory: Imagine your EYES slowly CLOSING after a long day until you peacefully FALL ASLEEP.

sentence.japanese: 昨日は あまり 眠れませんでした。
sentence.reading: きのうは あまり ねむれませんでした。

kunyomi: つと-める (tsuto-meru), つと-まる (tsuto-maru)
fact: The kanji 務 appears in many workplace words such as 勤務 (work duty), 業務 (business operations), and 義務 (obligation). It usually refers to responsibilities or duties that must be carried out.
memory: Imagine an OFFICE WORKER carefully completing every TASK on a long checklist. Finishing each responsibility reminds you of 務.

sentence.japanese: 今日は 夜まで 勤務します。
sentence.reading: きょうは よるまで きんむします。
sentence.romaji: kyou wa yoru made kinmu shimasu

fact: The kanji 夢 refers to both dreams while sleeping and lifelong ambitions. Japanese often say 『夢を叶える』, meaning 'to make your dream come true.'
memory: Imagine SLEEPING under the stars while dreaming about your FUTURE. Both nighttime dreams and life goals are 夢.

sentence.japanese: 私の 夢は 日本で 働くことです。
sentence.reading: わたしの ゆめは にほんで はたらくことです。
sentence.romaji: watashi no yume wa Nihon de hataraku koto desu
sentence.english: My dream is to work in Japan.

fact: The kanji 娘 commonly means 'daughter,' but it can also refer to a young woman. In anime and manga, you'll often hear words like 看板娘 (poster girl or shop girl).
memory: Imagine a LITTLE GIRL happily running to hug her parents. Their beloved DAUGHTER is 娘.

sentence.japanese: 娘は 来年 小学生に なります。
sentence.reading: むすめは らいねん しょうがくせいに なります。
sentence.romaji: musume wa rainen shougakusei ni narimasu
sentence.english: My daughter will become an elementary school student next year.

kunyomi: な-く (na-ku), な-る (na-ru), な-らす (na-rasu)
fact: The kanji 鳴 is used whenever something makes a sound. Birds 鳴く (sing), phones 鳴る (ring), and bells 鳴らす (ring something).
memory: Imagine a BIRD SINGING loudly while a nearby BELL RINGS. Every SOUND reminds you of 鳴.

sentence.japanese: 電話が 鳴っています。
sentence.reading: でんわが なっています。

fact: The kanji 命 is used in words like 生命 (life), 運命 (fate), and 命令 (command). Although its core meaning is 'life,' it also appears in words related to destiny and orders.
memory: Imagine a HEART beginning to beat inside a newborn baby. That precious LIFE is represented by 命.

sentence.japanese: 命は とても 大切です。
sentence.reading: いのちは とても たいせつです。

fact: The kanji 迷 appears in words like 迷子 (lost child), 迷惑 (trouble, inconvenience), and 迷信 (superstition). It always conveys the idea of losing one's way, physically or mentally.
memory: Imagine wandering through a HUGE MAZE with no exit in sight. You feel completely LOST and CONFUSED.

sentence.japanese: 駅で 道に 迷いました。
sentence.reading: えきで みちに まよいました。
sentence.english: I got lost near the station.

kunyomi: おも (omo), おもて (omote), つら (tsura)
fact: The kanji 面 is found in many everyday words such as 顔面 (face), 面接 (interview), and 面白い (interesting). Originally, it referred to a face or surface.
memory: Imagine looking at your FACE in a MIRROR. The smooth SURFACE reflects your expression.

sentence.japanese: その 映画は とても 面白かったです。
sentence.reading: その えいがは とても おもしろかったです。
sentence.romaji: sono eiga wa totemo omoshirokatta desu

fact: The kanji 約 is commonly used in two very different ways: 約束 (promise) and 約10分 (about 10 minutes). It can mean both 'agreement' and 'approximately.'
memory: Imagine SHAKING HANDS to make a PROMISE, then estimating you'll arrive in ABOUT ten minutes. Both ideas belong to 約.

sentence.japanese: 友達と 約束が あります。
sentence.reading: ともだちと やくそくが あります。
sentence.english: I have a promise with my friend.

fact: The kanji 役 appears in words like 役所 (government office), 主役 (leading role), and 役立つ (to be useful). It generally refers to someone's function or responsibility.
memory: Imagine an ACTOR stepping onto the stage to perform an important ROLE. Everyone has a different DUTY to play.

sentence.japanese: この 本は とても 役に立ちます。
sentence.reading: この ほんは とても やくに たちます。
sentence.romaji: kono hon wa totemo yaku ni tachimasu

fact: The kanji 遊 appears in words like 遊園地 (amusement park), 遊ぶ (to play), and 遊泳 (swimming for recreation). It is associated with leisure and enjoyment.
memory: Imagine CHILDREN PLAYING joyfully in a colorful PARK. Their laughter makes you think of fun and PLAY.

sentence.japanese: 週末は 友達と 遊びます。
sentence.reading: しゅうまつは ともだちと あそびます。
sentence.english: I'll hang out with my friends this weekend.

kunyomi: やさ-しい (yasa-shii), すぐ-れる (sugu-reru)
fact: The kanji 優 appears in words like 優勝 (championship victory), 優先 (priority), and 優秀 (excellent). It combines the ideas of kindness and excellence.
memory: Imagine a KIND teacher who is also the BEST in the school. Being both GENTLE and SUPERIOR reminds you of 優.

sentence.japanese: 彼女は とても 優しい 人です。
sentence.reading: かのじょは とても やさしい ひとです。
sentence.romaji: kanojo wa totemo yasashii hito desu
sentence.english: She is a very kind person.

fact: The kanji 予 appears in words like 予定 (schedule), 予習 (preparation for class), and 予約 (reservation). It carries the idea of doing something ahead of time.
memory: Imagine WRITING tomorrow's PLAN on your calendar TODAY. Preparing BEFOREHAND is the meaning of 予.

sentence.japanese: 旅行の 予定を 立てました。
sentence.reading: りょこうの よていを たてました。

fact: The kanji 余 appears in words like 余裕 (room to spare), 余分 (extra), and 余る (to remain). It refers to something left over or more than enough.
memory: Imagine FINISHING a meal but still having EXTRA FOOD left on your plate. That's the meaning of 余.

sentence.japanese: 時間が まだ 余っています。
sentence.reading: じかんが まだ あまっています。

fact: The kanji 与 appears in words like 与える (to give), 参加 (participation), and 給与 (salary). It often expresses the idea of giving, granting, or taking part.
memory: Imagine HANDING a PRESENT to a friend. You GIVE something valuable to another person.

sentence.japanese: 先生は 学生に 宿題を 与えました。
sentence.reading: せんせいは がくせいに しゅくだいを あたえました。
sentence.romaji: sensei wa gakusei ni shukudai o ataemashita
sentence.english: The teacher gave homework to the students.

fact: The kanji 容 appears in words like 内容 (contents), 容易 (easy), and 美容 (beauty care). Its original meaning relates to appearance or what something contains.
memory: Imagine OPENING a BOX to see everything INSIDE. The CONTENTS and the container's SHAPE remind you of 容.

sentence.japanese: この 本の 内容は とても 面白いです。
sentence.reading: この ほんの ないようは とても おもしろいです。
sentence.romaji: kono hon no naiyou wa totemo omoshiroi desu
sentence.english: The contents of this book are very interesting.

fact: The kanji 要 appears in many common words such as 必要 (necessary), 重要 (important), and 要点 (main point). It always conveys the idea of something essential or required.
memory: Imagine a CHECKLIST with one item circled in red because it is absolutely ESSENTIAL. You NEED it before you can continue.

sentence.japanese: パスポートが 必要です。
sentence.reading: パスポートが ひつようです。

fact: The kanji 様 is widely used as the respectful suffix '〜様,' as in お客様 (customer) and 神様 (god). It also appears in 様子 (condition or situation).
memory: Imagine bowing respectfully to an IMPORTANT GUEST and addressing them as 'SAMA.' That polite respect is captured by 様.

sentence.japanese: お客様が 来ました。
sentence.reading: おきゃくさまが きました。

fact: The kanji 欲 appears in words like 欲しい (want), 食欲 (appetite), and 意欲 (motivation). It expresses a strong desire for something.
memory: Imagine reaching toward your FAVORITE CAKE because you REALLY WANT it. That feeling of DESIRE is 欲.

sentence.japanese: 新しい パソコンが 欲しいです。
sentence.reading: あたらしい パソコンが ほしいです。

kunyomi: たの-む (tano-mu), たの-もしい (tano-moshii), たよ-る (tayo-ru)
fact: The kanji 頼 appears in common expressions like お願いします (request), 頼む (to ask), and 信頼 (trust). It expresses depending on someone or asking for help.
memory: Imagine ASKING your best friend for HELP because you TRUST them. Depending on someone reminds you of 頼.

sentence.japanese: 友達に 手伝いを 頼みました。
sentence.reading: ともだちに てつだいを たのみました。
sentence.english: I asked my friend for help.

fact: The kanji 利 appears in words like 便利 (convenient), 利用 (use), and 利益 (profit). It generally refers to advantage, usefulness, or benefit.
memory: Imagine finding a SHORTCUT that saves both TIME and MONEY. That ADVANTAGE or BENEFIT is 利.

sentence.japanese: 電車を 利用します。
sentence.reading: でんしゃを りようします。

fact: The kanji 流 appears in words like 一流 (first-class), 流行 (trend), and 流れる (to flow). Whether referring to water, fashion, or ideas, it carries the sense of movement.
memory: Imagine a RIVER flowing smoothly toward the sea. The moving WATER reminds you of FLOW and STREAM.

sentence.japanese: 川が ゆっくり 流れています。
sentence.reading: かわが ゆっくり ながれています。

fact: The kanji 留 appears in words like 留学 (study abroad), 保留 (pending), and 留守 (absence from home). It often conveys the idea of staying or remaining in one place.
memory: Imagine deciding to STAY in Japan for another year instead of going home. Remaining in one place reminds you of 留.

sentence.japanese: 彼は 日本へ 留学しました。
sentence.reading: かれは にほんへ りゅうがくしました。
sentence.romaji: kare wa Nihon e ryuugaku shimashita

fact: The kanji 両 appears in words like 両親 (parents), 両方 (both), and 車両 (railway car). It always refers to two sides or both of something.
memory: Imagine holding TWO APPLES, one in EACH HAND. Having BOTH sides filled reminds you of 両.

sentence.japanese: 両親は 元気です。
sentence.reading: りょうしんは げんきです。

fact: The kanji 良 appears in words like 良い (good), 良心 (conscience), and 改良 (improvement). It expresses goodness, quality, or improvement.
memory: Imagine receiving a GOLD STAR for doing GOOD work. Being EXCELLENT reminds you of 良.

sentence.japanese: 今日は 天気が 良いです。
sentence.reading: きょうは てんきが よいです。
sentence.romaji: kyou wa tenki ga yoi desu

fact: The kanji 類 appears in words like 人類 (humanity), 種類 (kind), and 書類 (documents). It is commonly used when grouping things into categories or types.
memory: Imagine SORTING books into different CATEGORIES on a shelf. Each TYPE belongs in its own group.

sentence.japanese: この 店には 多くの 種類が あります。
sentence.reading: この みせには おおくの しゅるいが あります。
sentence.romaji: kono mise ni wa ooku no shurui ga arimasu
sentence.english: This store has many kinds of products.

fact: The kanji 例 appears in common words like 例えば (for example), 例文 (example sentence), and 例外 (exception). It is one of the most useful kanji when explaining ideas.
memory: Imagine a TEACHER writing an EXAMPLE on the blackboard before giving the real test. That sample is 例.

sentence.japanese: 例を 一つ 教えてください。
sentence.reading: れいを ひとつ おしえてください。

kunyomi: つめ-たい (tsume-tai), ひ-える (hi-eru), ひ-やす (hi-yasu), さ-める (sa-meru), さ-ます (sa-masu)
fact: The kanji 冷 appears in words like 冷蔵庫 (refrigerator), 冷静 (calm), and 冷たい (cold). Besides temperature, it can also describe a calm or emotionally distant attitude.
memory: Imagine holding an ICE CUBE in your hand. Its icy touch reminds you of something COLD.

sentence.japanese: 今日は 水が とても 冷たいです。
sentence.reading: きょうは みずが とても つめたいです。
sentence.romaji: kyou wa mizu ga totemo tsumetai desu
sentence.english: The water is very cold today.

fact: The kanji 礼 is deeply connected to Japanese culture. It appears in words like お礼 (thanks), 礼儀 (manners), and 敬礼 (salute), reflecting the importance of politeness and respect.
memory: Imagine BOWING politely while saying 'Thank you.' That respectful gesture represents 礼.

sentence.japanese: 先生に お礼を 言いました。
sentence.reading: せんせいに おれいを いいました。

fact: The kanji 列 appears in words like 行列 (queue), 列車 (train), and 一列 (one line). It refers to people or objects arranged in an orderly row.
memory: Imagine PEOPLE standing in a LONG LINE waiting for a popular restaurant. That neat ROW is 列.

sentence.japanese: みんな 列に 並んでいます。
sentence.reading: みんな れつに ならんでいます。

kunyomi: つら-なる (tsura-naru), つ-れる (tsu-reru), つら-ねる (tsura-neru)
fact: The kanji 連 appears in words like 連絡 (contact), 連続 (continuation), and 関連 (related). It expresses the idea of things being connected or linked together.
memory: Imagine several CHAINS linked together to form one LONG CONNECTION. Every link is connected to the next.

sentence.japanese: あとで 連絡します。
sentence.reading: あとで れんらくします。

fact: The kanji 路 appears in words like 道路 (road), 通路 (passage), and 路線 (route). It refers to roads, paths, and transportation routes.
memory: Imagine following a LONG ROAD that stretches toward the mountains. The ROUTE guides you to your destination.

sentence.japanese: この 道路は とても 広いです。
sentence.reading: この どうろは とても ひろいです。
sentence.romaji: kono douro wa totemo hiroi desu

fact: The kanji 労 appears in words like 労働 (labor), 苦労 (hardship), and 疲労 (fatigue). It is closely associated with work, effort, and the results of hard labor.
memory: Imagine a WORKER wiping sweat from their forehead after a long day's LABOR. Their HARD WORK represents 労.

sentence.japanese: 毎日の 労働は 大変です。
sentence.reading: まいにちの ろうどうは たいへんです。
sentence.romaji: mainichi no roudou wa taihen desu

fact: The kanji 老 appears in words like 老人 (elderly person), 老舗 (long-established shop), and 老後 (old age). It generally refers to aging, seniority, or long experience.
memory: Imagine an ELDERLY GRANDPARENT walking slowly with a cane. Their many years of life remind you of 老.

sentence.japanese: 人は いつか 老います。
sentence.reading: ひとは いつか おいます。

fact: The kanji 論 appears in words like 議論 (discussion), 理論 (theory), and 結論 (conclusion). It is commonly used in academic writing and debates.
memory: Imagine STUDENTS debating different IDEAS until they finally reach a CONCLUSION. That thoughtful discussion represents 論.

sentence.japanese: その 問題について 議論しました。
sentence.reading: その もんだいについて ぎろんしました。
sentence.romaji: sono mondai ni tsuite giron shimashita

kunyomi: やわ-らぐ (yawa-ragu), なご-む (nago-mu), なご-やか (nago-yaka)
fact: The kanji 和 symbolizes harmony and is strongly associated with traditional Japanese culture. It appears in words like 平和 (peace), 和食 (Japanese cuisine), and 和室 (Japanese-style room).
memory: Imagine FRIENDS sharing a traditional Japanese meal together in perfect HARMONY. Their peaceful atmosphere reminds you of 和.

sentence.japanese: 私は 和食が 大好きです。
sentence.reading: わたしは わしょくが だいすきです。
sentence.romaji: watashi wa washoku ga daisuki desu

fact: The kanji 身 appears in many everyday words such as 身体 (body), 中身 (contents), and 身分 (social status). It often refers to one's body, self, or personal identity.
memory: Imagine looking at your WHOLE BODY in a mirror. Your BODY represents your true SELF, making it easy to remember 身.

sentence.japanese: 健康のために 身体を 動かします。
sentence.reading: けんこうのために しんたいを うごかします。
sentence.romaji: kenkou no tame ni shintai o ugokashimasu

fact: The kanji 的 originally meant 'target' in archery, but today it is far more commonly used as the suffix '-like' or '-al' in words such as 日本的 (Japanese-style) and 具体的 (concrete).
memory: Imagine an ARCHER aiming at a TARGET. Hitting the TARGET reminds you of 的.

sentence.japanese: 旅行の 目的は 観光です。
sentence.reading: りょこうの もくてきは かんこうです。
sentence.romaji: ryokou no mokuteki wa kankou desu
sentence.english: The purpose of the trip is sightseeing.

fact: The kanji 落 appears in words like 落ちる (to fall), 落とす (to drop), and 落第 (failing an exam). It can describe physical falling as well as losing status or failing.
memory: Imagine an APPLE falling from a TREE and DROPPING onto the ground. That sudden FALL is 落.

sentence.japanese: 財布を 落としてしまいました。
sentence.reading: さいふを おとしてしまいました。

fact: The kanji 陽 appears in words like 太陽 (sun), 陽気 (cheerful), and 陽だまり (sunlit spot). It represents sunshine, warmth, and positive energy.
memory: Imagine standing in WARM SUNLIGHT on a bright spring morning. The SUNNY feeling reminds you of 陽.

sentence.japanese: 今日は 太陽が とても 明るいです。
sentence.reading: きょうは たいようが とても あかるいです。
sentence.romaji: kyou wa taiyou ga totemo akarui desu
sentence.english: The sun is very bright today.

onyomi: キュウ (kyuu), グウ (guu), ク (ku)
fact: The kanji 宮 appears in famous place names like 伊勢神宮 (Ise Grand Shrine) and 皇居's 宮殿 (Imperial Palace). It refers to shrines, palaces, and grand residences.
memory: Imagine walking through the grand gates of a ROYAL PALACE beside a magnificent SHRINE. Both are represented by 宮.

sentence.japanese: 京都で 有名な 神宮を 訪れました。
sentence.reading: きょうとで ゆうめいな じんぐうを おとずれました。
sentence.romaji: Kyouto de yuumei na jinguu o otozuremashita
sentence.english: I visited a famous shrine in Kyoto.

fact: The kanji 救 appears in words like 救急車 (ambulance), 救助 (rescue), and 救世主 (savior). It represents helping or saving someone in danger.
memory: Imagine a LIFEGUARD pulling someone out of the water just in time. That heroic RESCUE is 救.

sentence.japanese: 救急車を 呼んでください。
sentence.reading: きゅうきゅうしゃを よんでください。

fact: The kanji 級 is commonly used for rankings and proficiency levels, such as JLPT N5級, 一級 (first class), and 高級 (high-class).
memory: Imagine climbing a LADDER where each step is a higher GRADE or LEVEL. Every step brings you to a new 級.

sentence.japanese: 彼は 上級クラスで 勉強しています。
sentence.reading: かれは じょうきゅうクラスで べんきょうしています。
sentence.romaji: kare wa joukyuu kurasu de benkyou shiteimasu
sentence.english: He is studying in the advanced class.

fact: The kanji 寺 appears in the names of many famous Buddhist temples in Japan, such as 清水寺 (Kiyomizu-dera) and 金閣寺 (Kinkaku-ji).
memory: Imagine a PEACEFUL TEMPLE surrounded by cherry blossoms, where monks quietly ring a bell. That sacred place is 寺.

sentence.japanese: 京都で 有名な 寺を 見学しました。
sentence.reading: きょうとで ゆうめいな てらを けんがくしました。
sentence.romaji: Kyouto de yuumei na tera o kengaku shimashita
sentence.english: I visited a famous temple in Kyoto.

fact: The kanji 坂 is found in many Japanese place names such as 大阪 (Osaka) and 赤坂 (Akasaka). Japan's hilly landscape explains why this kanji appears so often.
memory: Imagine riding a bicycle UP a STEEP HILL. The uphill SLOPE reminds you of 坂.

sentence.japanese: この 坂は とても 急です。
sentence.reading: この さかは とても きゅうです。
sentence.romaji: kono saka wa totemo kyuu desu

fact: Japan is home to thousands of historic bridges. The kanji 橋 appears in famous landmarks like 日本橋 (Nihonbashi), which has served as the traditional starting point of Japan's major highways since the Edo period.
memory: Imagine crossing a WOODEN BRIDGE over a peaceful river to reach the other side. That crossing is 橋.

sentence.japanese: 橋を 渡って 駅へ 行きます。
sentence.reading: はしを わたって えきへ いきます。
sentence.romaji: hashi o watatte eki e ikimasu
sentence.english: I cross the bridge to go to the station.

fact: Japan consists of over 14,000 islands, although four main islands—Honshu, Hokkaido, Kyushu, and Shikoku—contain almost all of the country's population. It's no surprise that 島 appears in many Japanese place names.
memory: Imagine a lonely ISLAND surrounded by endless blue water with a single bird flying overhead. That isolated land is 島.

sentence.japanese: この 島は とても 美しいです。
sentence.reading: この しまは とても うつくしいです。
sentence.romaji: kono shima wa totemo utsukushii desu

fact: As an island nation, Japan has over 35,000 kilometers of coastline. The kanji 岸 appears in words like 海岸 (seacoast) and 対岸 (opposite shore).
memory: Imagine standing on the SHORE watching waves gently reach the COAST. The land beside the water is 岸.

sentence.japanese: 子どもたちは 岸で 遊んでいます。
sentence.reading: こどもたちは きしで あそんでいます。
sentence.english: The children are playing on the shore.

fact: Japan's largest lake, 琵琶湖 (Lake Biwa), supplies drinking water to millions of people in the Kansai region. The kanji 湖 is used for large lakes.
memory: Imagine a CALM LAKE reflecting snow-covered mountains like a giant mirror. That peaceful body of water is 湖.

sentence.japanese: 湖の 景色が とても きれいです。
sentence.reading: みずうみの けしきが とても きれいです。
sentence.romaji: mizuumi no keshiki ga totemo kirei desu
sentence.english: The scenery around the lake is very beautiful.

fact: Rice farming has shaped Japanese culture for over 2,000 years. The kanji 農 appears in words like 農業 (agriculture) and 農家 (farmer's family).
memory: Imagine a FARMER planting rice seedlings in a muddy field under the sun. That hard work is 農.

sentence.japanese: 祖父は 農業を しています。
sentence.reading: そふは のうぎょうを しています。

fact: As an island nation, Japan has one of the world's largest fishing industries. The kanji 漁 appears in words like 漁業 (fishing industry) and 漁船 (fishing boat).
memory: Imagine a FISHERMAN casting a large NET into the sea before sunrise. Catching fish for a living is 漁.

sentence.japanese: この 村では 漁業が 盛んです。
sentence.reading: この むらでは ぎょぎょうが さかんです。
sentence.romaji: kono mura de wa gyogyou ga sakan desu
sentence.english: Fishing is a major industry in this village.

fact: Traditional Japanese binchōtan charcoal is famous for burning with very little smoke and intense heat. It has been used for centuries in grilling, tea ceremonies, and even water purification.
memory: Imagine BLACK CHARCOAL glowing bright red in a barbecue grill. The burning COAL reminds you of 炭.

sentence.japanese: 炭火で 魚を 焼きました。
sentence.reading: すみびで さかなを やきました。

fact: The kanji 油 appears in many everyday words such as 石油 (petroleum), 醤油 (soy sauce), and 食用油 (cooking oil). Despite sharing the character, not all 'oils' are the same type.
memory: Imagine POURING COOKING OIL into a hot frying pan before making tempura. The shiny liquid reminds you of 油.

sentence.japanese: 油を 少し 入れてください。
sentence.reading: あぶらを すこし いれてください。

fact: Blood type personality is surprisingly popular in Japan. Many people casually ask someone's 血液型 (blood type), similar to asking about zodiac signs in other countries.
memory: Imagine a RED DROP OF BLOOD falling onto a white bandage. That bright red drop is 血.

sentence.japanese: 血が 少し 出ています。
sentence.reading: ちが すこし でています。

fact: The kanji 皮 is used for the outer layer of both people and objects. It appears in words like 皮膚 (skin), 果皮 (fruit peel), and 毛皮 (fur).
memory: Imagine PEELING an ORANGE by removing its SKIN. The peel represents 皮.

sentence.japanese: りんごの 皮を むきました。
sentence.reading: りんごの かわを むきました。

fact: The kanji 毛 appears in words like 毛糸 (wool yarn), 毛布 (blanket), and 羽毛 (feathers/down). It refers to hair, fur, or fine fibers on animals and materials.
memory: Imagine brushing SOFT HAIR on a fluffy cat. Its soft FUR reminds you of 毛.

sentence.japanese: 犬の 毛が 長いです。
sentence.reading: いぬの けが ながいです。
sentence.romaji: inu no ke ga nagai desu

fact: The kanji 帽 is almost always seen in compounds such as 帽子 (hat). During Japan's Meiji era, Western-style hats became popular, and 帽子 entered everyday vocabulary.
memory: Imagine putting a BIG HAT on your head before walking into the hot summer sun. The hat protects you, reminding you of 帽.

sentence.japanese: 暑いので 帽子を かぶります。
sentence.reading: あついので ぼうしを かぶります。
sentence.english: I wear a hat because it's hot.

fact: Japan has produced high-quality silk for centuries. During the Meiji period, raw silk became one of Japan's most valuable exports to the world.
memory: Imagine touching a SOFT SILK scarf that feels incredibly smooth. That luxurious fabric is 絹.

sentence.japanese: この 着物は 絹で できています。
sentence.reading: この きものは きぬで できています。
sentence.romaji: kono kimono wa kinu de dekiteimasu
sentence.english: This kimono is made of silk.

fact: The kanji 綿 is used for both cotton fiber and stuffing. It appears in words like 木綿 (cotton cloth) and 綿布 (cotton fabric).
memory: Imagine holding a SOFT BALL OF COTTON that feels light as a cloud. That fluffy material is 綿.

sentence.japanese: 綿の シャツは 着心地が 良いです。
sentence.reading: わたの シャツは きごこちが いいです。
sentence.romaji: wata no shatsu wa kigokochi ga ii desu
sentence.english: Cotton shirts are comfortable to wear.

fact: Traditional Japanese festivals often feature the sound of the bamboo flute, called the 篠笛 (shinobue), which has been played for hundreds of years.
memory: Imagine playing a BAMBOO FLUTE that fills the air with beautiful music. That instrument is 笛.

sentence.japanese: 子どもが 笛を 吹いています。
sentence.reading: こどもが ふえを ふいています。
sentence.english: The child is playing the flute.

fact: Every New Year's Eve in Japan, Buddhist temples ring a giant bell 108 times in a tradition called 除夜の鐘 (Joya no Kane), symbolizing the removal of 108 worldly desires.
memory: Imagine a HUGE TEMPLE BELL echoing across the mountains at midnight. That powerful bell is 鐘.

sentence.japanese: 寺の 鐘が 鳴っています。
sentence.reading: てらの かねが なっています。

fact: Japan holds thousands of traditional festivals (祭り) every year. Famous events like Kyoto's Gion Matsuri and Aomori's Nebuta Matsuri have been celebrated for centuries and often honor local shrines or seasonal traditions.
memory: Imagine colorful FESTIVAL floats, fireworks, and people in yukata dancing through the streets. That joyful CELEBRATION is 祭.

sentence.japanese: 夏祭りへ 友達と 行きました。
sentence.reading: なつまつりへ ともだちと いきました。
sentence.english: I went to the summer festival with my friends.

fact: At Japanese shrines, visitors often clap twice, bow, and quietly make a prayer. The kanji 祈 appears in words like 祈願 (prayer for success) and 祈る (to pray).
memory: Imagine folding your HANDS before a shrine and making a heartfelt WISH. That act of PRAYING is 祈.

sentence.japanese: 家族の 健康を 祈っています。
sentence.reading: かぞくの けんこうを いのっています。
sentence.english: I pray for my family's health.

fact: The kanji 祝 is commonly seen on wedding cards, birthday messages, and signs saying 'Congratulations!' In Japan, the character itself often symbolizes celebration.
memory: Imagine throwing CONFETTI while shouting 'CONGRATULATIONS!' at a friend's graduation. That's the meaning of 祝.

sentence.japanese: みんなで 誕生日を 祝いました。
sentence.reading: みんなで たんじょうびを いわいました。

fact: The kanji 舎 appears in words like 校舎 (school building), 宿舎 (lodging), and 寄宿舎 (dormitory). It refers to buildings where people live, study, or stay.
memory: Imagine a SCHOOL BUILDING filled with students studying together. That large BUILDING is 舎.

sentence.japanese: 新しい 校舎が 完成しました。
sentence.reading: あたらしい こうしゃが かんせいしました。
sentence.english: The new school building has been completed.

kunyomi: か (ka), かお-り (kao-ri), かお-る (kao-ru)
fact: Japan has a traditional art called 香道 (Kōdō), the 'Way of Incense,' where participants appreciate and identify different incense fragrances much like a tea ceremony.
memory: Imagine the SWEET FRAGRANCE of cherry blossoms mixed with INCENSE drifting through a quiet temple. That pleasant SCENT is 香.

sentence.japanese: 花の 香りが 好きです。
sentence.reading: はなの かおりが すきです。
sentence.romaji: hana no kaori ga suki desu
sentence.english: I like the fragrance of flowers.

fact: Beans play an important role in Japanese culture. During the Setsubun festival, people throw roasted soybeans while shouting '鬼は外! 福は内!' ('Demons out! Good fortune in!') to bring good luck.
memory: Imagine a SMALL BEAN bouncing across the floor during a Japanese festival. That tiny bean reminds you of 豆.

sentence.japanese: 私は 納豆が 好きです。
sentence.reading: わたしは なっとうが すきです。
sentence.romaji: watashi wa nattou ga suki desu

fact: Barley tea (麦茶, mugicha) is one of Japan's most popular summer drinks. Unlike regular tea, it contains no caffeine and is commonly served cold in homes and restaurants.
memory: Imagine a GOLDEN WHEAT FIELD swaying gently in the wind before harvest. Those tall stalks remind you of 麦.

sentence.japanese: 夏は 冷たい 麦茶を よく 飲みます。
sentence.reading: なつは つめたい むぎちゃを よく のみます。
sentence.romaji: natsu wa tsumetai mugicha o yoku nomimasu
sentence.english: I often drink cold barley tea in summer.
